comics.title: Convergent Subsequence
comics.transcript:
# Scene
A minimalist webcomic showing a simple character with a round head and blue cap watching a sunset. The art uses bold colors—deep blue sky, yellow and orange sunset gradients, and a silhouetted figure—rendered in simple line drawings. The layout progresses from one large panel to increasingly smaller panels, visually representing the character's thoughts becoming more introspective and fragmented.

# Text

Panel 1: "SOMETIMES, WHEN I'M ALONE WATCHING A SUNSET..."

Panel 2: "...I FEEL LIKE I'M WATCHING THE GREAT SEA OF POSSIBILITY..."

Panel 3: "...AND SOMETIMES I BELIEVE THAT I CAN SEE THE WHOLE SEQUENCE OF MY LIFE BEFORE ME..."

Panel 4: "...MEANDERING AIMLESSLY LIKE AN ABSTRUSE GOOSE..."

Panel 5: "...SEARCHING FOR FULFILLMENT IN THIS CORRUPT SUBSET OF EXISTENCE."

Panel 6: "MY LIFE MUST HAVE A CONVERGENT SUBSEQUENCE SOMEWHERE... OUT THERE..."

Panel 7-8: "WHERE THERE'S A HIGHER FUNCTION..." / "WHY CAN'T I PROVE IT?" / [Additional text becomes illegible in increasingly tiny panels]

# Visual note
The progressively shrinking panel sizes mirror the character's mental spiral into increasingly abstract and obsessive philosophical questioning, creating a visual representation of overthinking.

comics.title: SETI Finally Receives a Signal
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A black and white stick figure comic set at headquarters, showing two characters at a computer workstation. One sits at a desk with a monitor and keyboard while the other stands nearby.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
MEANWHILE, AT SET 1 HEADQUARTERS...

**Panel 2:**
?!?
BEEP BEEP BEEP

**Panel 3:**
A SIGNAL! WE GOT A SIGNAL!!!
BEEP BEEP BEEP

**Panel 4:**
IT'S DEFINITELY NONRANDOM. IT APPEARS TO BE COMING FROM THE PLEIADES CLUSTER.
WOW!
BEEP

**Panel 5:**
IT LOOKS LIKE A COMPRESSED BINARY ENCODING OF A PRESSURE WAVE.
YOU'RE RIGHT!
BEEP

**Panel 6:**
LET'S SEE IF WE CAN DECODE IT WITH ANY OF OUR STANDARD AUDIO CODECS.
BEEP

**Panel 7:**
THERE.

**Panel 8:**
CRACKLE...

**Panel 9:**
THIS HAS GONE TOO FAR.
♪ NEVER GONNA GIVE YOU UP ♪ NEVER GONNA LET YOU DOWN... ♪

# Visual note

The alien signal decodes to the opening lyrics of "Never Gonna Give You Up," a famous internet meme.

comics.title: LOLCAT Backlash
comics.transcript:
# Comic Transcription

**Scene:** A nine-panel minimalist black-and-white comic featuring a person and a cat in an indoor setting. The art style uses simple line drawings with expressive character reactions.

**Text:**

Panel 1: (No text)

Panel 2: Person: "TACT YOU IZ IT?"

Panel 3: Person: "I CAN HAS CHEEZBURGER? HEE HEE..."

Panel 4: Person: "AH TINK AH SMELLS ME A CHEEZBURGER."

Panel 5: Person: "I HELP WIF UR SHOPPING. OWNA PLEASE. LOLCATS 5000 LAST YEAR."

Panel 6: Person: "I BIG SCARY MONSTER. RAAAAAH!"
Cat: "EVERYDAY... THE SAME SHIT."

Panel 7: Person: "Y DEFI TREEZ ON DAT PLATE? LAUGH WHILE YOU CAN, MONKEY BOY."

Panel 8: Person: "HAVE YOU NOTICED I DON'T USE THE LITTER BOX ANYMORE?"
Cat: "U KANT HAVE MY TOEZ."

Panel 9: Person: "GUESS WHO SLEEPS WITH HIS MOUTH OPEN!"
Cat: "IZ MAH HOUSE I"

**Visual note:** The humor relies on the person's increasingly absurd impersonations using cat meme speech patterns, while the cat responds with mounting frustration and deadpan commentary.

comics.title: There is No Spoon
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist stick figure comic with two round-headed characters. The first character attempts to bend a spoon through mental willpower while the second character observes, then intervenes.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"THERE IS NO SPOON.
THERE IS NO SPOON.
THERE IS NO SPOON.
THERE IS NO SPOON."

**Panel 2:**
"I'M TESTING A THEORY ON MIND."

**Panel 3:**
"I BELIEVE THAT I CAN USE MY MIND TO PRODUCE A QUANTUM GRAVITATIONAL LENSING EFFECT TO BEND THE SPOON'S SUPER-POSED QUANTUM STATES TO MY DESIRED SPACE-TIME GEOMETRY CONFIGURATION."

**Panel 4:**
"IN EFFECT, I'M USING MY MIND TO FORCE THE UNIVERSE TO BEND THE SPOON."

**Panel 5:**
"?!"

**Panel 6:**
"SWITCH"

**Panel 7:**
[No dialogue]

**Panel 8:**
"HARRPH!"

**Panel 9:**
"THAT STILL COUNTS!
TOTALLY!"

# Visual Note

The second character secretly swaps the original spoon with an already-bent spoon while the first character strains to use mental willpower, creating the illusion that the mind-bending experiment succeeded.

comics.title: Schrödinger's Infinitesimal Miscalculation
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A six-panel black and white comic in simple line-art style featuring a stick-figure character with glasses. The setting is indoors with minimal furniture. The comic depicts a sequence involving the character and a cat in a box.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"...1935"
|ψ⟩ = ½|LIVE⟩ + ½|DEAD⟩ + ½|CAT⟩

**Panel 2:**
"MEOW"

**Panel 3:**
(no text)

**Panel 4:**
(no text)

**Panel 5:**
"!!"

**Panel 6:**
½|LIVE⟩ + ½|DEAD⟩ + |ε⟩|ANGRY⟩|HUMAN⟩

# Visual Note

The comic references Schrödinger's cat thought experiment. When the character opens the box and observes the living cat, the equation updates to replace the superposition with a definite state that includes an angry human component.

comics.title: Schrödinger's Miscalculation - Part 2
comics.transcript:
# Scene
A minimalist stick figure comic in black and white depicting a character and a box across six panels. The narrative moves from a physics equation to a cat's complaint about being confined.

# Text
Panel 1: "...1935" / "|ψ⟩ = ½|LIVE⟩ + ½|DEAD⟩ + ½|CAT⟩"

Panel 2: "MEOW"

Panel 5: "WHAT FOR U HIDE ME IN DARK COLD BOX?"

Panel 6: "½|LIVE⟩/√|CAT⟩ + ½|DEAD⟩/√|CAT⟩ + ½|LOL⟩/√|CAT⟩"

# Visual Note
The final equation introduces "|LOL⟩" as a third quantum state, adding a humorous component to the original thought experiment formula.

comics.title: Arguing With a String Theorist
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A comic strip with eight panels featuring two simple round-faced characters in black and white line art. One character delivers increasingly technical criticisms of string theory while the other becomes progressively frustrated, culminating in a sudden violent outburst.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"...FIRST OF ALL, STRING THEORY HAS NOT MADE A SINGLE TESTABLE PREDICTION IN OVER 30 YEARS."

**Panel 2:**
"BY MAKING STRING THEORY RIDICULOUSLY MALLEABLE WITH YOUR 10TH DIFFERENT WAYS TO COMPACTIFY THE EXTRA DIMENSIONS..."

**Panel 3:**
"...YOU ESSENTIALLY PUT THE THEORY BEYOND THE REACH OF ANY CONCEIVABLE EXPERIMENTAL TEST."

**Panel 4:**
"SECOND OF ALL, STRING THEORY IS ONLY FORMULATED PERTURBATIVELY. A FULL NON-PERTURBATIVE DEFINITION OF THE THEORY DOESN'T EXIST."

**Panel 5:**
"THIRD, STRING THEORY DESCRIBES PERTURBATIVE EXPANSIONS ABOUT FIXED SPACETIME BACKGROUNDS."

**Panel 6:**
"ANY RESPECTABLE FUNDAMENTAL THEORY OF QUANTUM GRAVITY WOULD BE BACKGROUND INDEPENDENT."

**Panel 7:**
"FOURTH, IN STRING THEORY, THE DIRAC OPERATOR AND THE GAUGE FIELDS ARE..."

**Panel 8:**
"PAK"

**Panel 9:**
"YOU KEEP TALKING LIKE A BITCH, I'M GONNA SLAP YOU LIKE A BITCH."

# Visual Note

The humor depends on the abrupt tonal shift from six panels of calm, technical physics criticism to a sudden violent physical response accompanied by a slapping sound effect.

comics.title: Blind Date
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist stick-figure webcomic. Two characters sit at a table: one with long black hair on the left, one with a round head wearing blue on the right. The comic progresses through nine panels showing their conversation, with thought bubbles appearing in later panels.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Character 1: "SO TELL ME ABOUT YOURSELF."

**Panel 2:**
Character 2: "THIS SILVERWARE ARRANGEMENT REMINDS ME OF THE BINARY REPRESENTATION OF 2110 WHICH IS EXACTLY THE NUMBER OF SYMMETRY GROUPS POSSIBLE IN A CRYSTAL LATTICE."

**Panel 3:**
Character 1: "ACTUALLY, THERE ARE 230 SYMMETRY GROUPS IN 3D IF YOU CONSIDER ENANTIOMORPHOUS CHARACTER."

**Panel 4:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 5:**
Character 2: "FWAP!"

**Panel 6-7:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 8:**
Character 1: "I ENJOY LONG WALKS IN THE PARK."

**Panel 9:**
Character 2: "ME TOO."

# Visual note

Character 2 slaps themselves in panel 5 and exits. Hearts and musical notes float in panels 6-7, indicating Character 2 returns when given a normal conversation topic.

comics.title: The Red Button
comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription

**Scene:** A minimalist stick-figure comic set in heaven. God, a larger figure, instructs Gabriel, a smaller angelic figure, at a control panel with buttons and dials. The art uses black ink on white with occasional yellow highlights.

**Text:**

Panel 1:
Caption: "...meanwhile, in heaven..."
God: "GABRIEL!"
Gabriel: "YES, LORD."

Panel 2:
God: "I HAVE BUSINESS IN ANOTHER UNIVERSE. I NEED YOU TO MONITOR THE FINE-TUNING MACHINE."
Gabriel: "YES LORD"

Panel 3:
God: "THESE BUTTONS CONTROL THE FORCE CONSTANTS AND THESE DIALS ADJUST THE PARTICLE MASS RATIOS. IT'S PRETTY SELF-EXPLANATORY."

Panel 4:
God: "BUT WHATEVER YOU DO NEVER PRESS THE RED BUTTON!"
Gabriel: "YES, LORD."

Panel 5:
God: "I'LL BE BACK IN A FEW EONS."
Gabriel: "YES, LORD."

Panel 6:
Caption: "...eons later."

Panels 7-9:
[Gabriel stares at the red button, presses it, and causes an explosion]

**Visual note:** Gabriel immediately presses the forbidden red button once God leaves, triggering an explosion—a visual joke on the irresistible temptation of the forbidden and the consequences for universal fine-tuning.

comics.title: Ubuntu Sucks
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A nine-panel comic strip in black and white line art showing a person sitting at a desk with a computer. The person has short dark hair and appears increasingly distressed throughout the strip. The art style is simple and sketch-like, with minimal details.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
[Code visible on screen: FFA 33 CO BE DO BC 00 7C / 8B F4 50 07 50 1F FB FC / BF 00 06 B1 00 01 F2 BC J]

**Panel 2:**
[Musical notation visible on screen]
★ HELLO ★

**Panel 3:**
SOMETHING FEELS DIFFERENT TODAY.

**Panel 4:**
MY BED STORE FEELS FUNNY.

**Panel 5:**
JESUS CHRIST! MY MAIN PARTITION HAS BEEN RESIZED! OH NO YOU DIDN'T?!
← STALL →

**Panel 6:**
WHO'S BEING A SLUGGISH LITTLE VISTA MACHINE TODAY? YOU ARE, YES YOU ARE.

**Panel 7:**
DON'T PATRONIZE ME YOU TWO-TIMING DUAL-BOOTER.

**Panel 8:**
I CAN SMELL THE HARDY HERON ON MY HARD DRIVE.

**Panel 9:**
TURN AWAY. I CAN'T BEAR TO LOOK AT YOU RIGHT NOW.

# Visual Note

The humor relies on the person anthropomorphizing their computer and treating it like an unfaithful partner discovering evidence of dual-booting. Technical computer terms like partition resizing, Vista, Hardy Heron, and dual-booting are used as metaphors for relationship betrayal.

comics.title: Math Text
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A multi-panel webcomic featuring a character at a computer desk surrounded by mathematics textbooks. The art style uses simple line drawing with minimal detail, and the character's expressions shift from suspicion to frustration throughout the strip.

# Text

Panel 1: "I'VE ALWAYS SUSPECTED THAT THIS IS HOW MATHEMATICS TEXTS ARE WRITTEN."

Panel 2: "Define a point p in a metric space X to be a *condensation point* of a set E ⊂ X if every neighborhood of p contains uncountably many points of E."

Panel 3: "Suppose E ⊂ ℝ^k, E is uncountable, and let P be the set of all condensation points of E."

Panel 4: "THEOREM: P is perfect and P^c ∩ E is at most countable."

Panel 5: "PROOF OF THEOREM:"

Panel 6: "HONEY, LOOK WHAT I'M WEARING."

Panel 7: "FWAP"

Panel 8: "DAMN YOU, WALTER RUDIN... ...AND GODSPEED."

Bottom right: "Left as exercise."

# Visual note

The character ignores their partner's attempt at seduction while absorbed in reading a mathematics textbook, then throws the book in frustration. The phrase "Left as exercise" refers to the common textbook practice of omitting proof steps for readers to complete themselves.

comics.title: I Never
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A stick-figure style webcomic with four characters—one in blue, three in gray—arranged in a group. Each panel shows the blue character making confessions while the others react with increasing exasperation, indicated by laughter and drinking sounds.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"I NEVER MEMORIZED PI TO MORE THAN 1000 DIGITS WHILE WAITING FOR MY CODE TO COMPILE."

**Panel 2:**
"SWIG SWIG SWIG"
"HA HA HA HA"

**Panel 3:**
"OK, OK, I'M NEXT!"

**Panel 4:**
"I NEVER IGNORED MY THESIS PROJECT FOR WEEKS WHILE SECRETLY TRYING TO PROVE THE RIEMANN HYPOTHESIS."
"SWIG SWIG SWIG"

**Panel 5:**
"HA HA HA HA"
"MY TURN, MY TURN!"

**Panel 6:**
"I NEVER SNORTED A LINE OF CAKE OFF OF A STRIPPER'S ASS AT CHUCK'S BACHELOR PARTY."

**Panel 7:**
"SWIG"

**Panel 8:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 9:**
"WHAT, I THOUGHT WE WERE TELLING STUFF."

# Visual Note

The humor escalates from relatable programmer confessions to shockingly inappropriate behavior. The other characters' silence in response to the final confession, followed by the blue character's confused reaction, creates the comedic payoff.

comics.title: The Opportunist
comics.transcript:
**Scene:**
A four-panel stick figure comic showing two people seated at opposite sides of a table with a pizza between them. The art style is minimalist with simple black line drawings on white background, and one character wears glasses.

**Text:**

Panel 1:
"SOME PEOPLE JUST CAN'T RESIST..."

Panel 2:
"* GCRK *"
"* CHOKE *"
"?"
"!"

Panel 3:
"* ACKK *"
"* CHOKE *"

Panel 4:
"I FIND YOUR LACK OF FAITH... DISTURBING."
"* GAG! *"

**Visual note:**
The comic plays on the double meaning of "choke"—Character 1 is literally choking on pizza while Character 2 delivers a line about lack of faith, suggesting Character 1 lacked confidence in their eating ability.

comics.title: dckx
comics.transcript:
# How Electric Skateboards Work

**Scene:**
A four-panel stick-figure comic in black ink on white background depicting a narrative about electric skateboards.

**Text:**

Panel 1:
"MY HOBBY: READING COMICS BACKWARDS. SOMETIMES IT'S FUNNIER THAT WAY. BUT SOMETIMES IT JUST MAKES ME SAD."

Panel 2:
"POINT B"
(Multiple stick figures labeled "CHICKS" gathered around a figure on an electric skateboard)

Panel 3:
"WHIRRRR"
(Stick figure on skateboard with motion lines)

Panel 4:
"POINT A"
(Solitary stick figure on skateboard)

**Visual note:**
The comic's humor depends on reversed reading order—when read backwards, the sequence reverses causality to suggest electric skateboards repel people rather than attract them.

comics.title: The Birth of ENIAC
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A multi-panel educational comic in simple black-and-white stick figure style with occasional color highlights. The comic presents historical facts about ENIAC through a conversational exchange between stick figures, culminating in a modern twist where the figures use a computer for entertainment.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA, 1946...
ENIAC
IT CONTAINED 17,468 VACUUM TUBES, 7,200 CRYSTAL DIODES, 1,500 RELAYS, 70,000 RESISTORS, AND 10,000 CAPACITORS.

**Panel 2:**
ENIAC
ENIAC WAS THE WORLD'S FIRST GENERAL-PURPOSE ELECTRONIC COMPUTER.
ENIAC WAS PROGRAMMED BY LABORIOUSLY MANIPULATING SWITCHES AND CABLES BY HAND. EVEN SIMPLE CALCULATIONS TOOK DAYS TO PROGRAM.

**Panel 3:**
ENIAC
IT WEIGHED 30 TONS AND TOOK UP 680 SQUARE FEET OF SPACE.
IT CONSUMED 150W OF POWER AND EVERY TIME IT WAS SWITCHED ON, THE STREET LIGHTS OF PHILADELPHIA FLICKERED.

**Panel 4:**
IT TOOK ALMOST 3 YEARS TO BUILD AND IT COST OVER $500,000.

**Panel 5:**
ENIAC
THE ENGINEERS WERE LATER QUOTED AS SAYING...

**Panel 6:**
...TOTALLY WORTH IT, BITCHES!

# Visual Note

The final panel shows the figures playing a video game on a television, depicting a use case that would have been incomprehensible to ENIAC's creators, emphasizing how far computing technology has advanced.

comics.title: I.I. Rabi's Question Answered?
comics.transcript:
# Scene
A six-panel stick-figure comic in black and white. The setting transitions from heaven, where God and angels prepare to create the universe, to Earth 15 billion years in the future, where humans struggle with particle physics equations.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Caption: "...in heaven..."
God: "ARE WE READY TO START CREATING THE UNIVERSE?"
Angels: "YES, LORD."

**Panel 2:**
God: "WE'VE FINISHED THE FORCE CARRIER CALCULATIONS. NOW WE NEED TO START ADDING LEPTONS."

**Panel 3:**
God: "HERE'S THE INVOICE FROM OUR LAST SHIPMENT FROM US."
Label: "UNIVERSE PARTICLE SERVICES LTD"

**Panel 4:**
God: "ELECTRON, ELECTRON NEUTRINO, TAU, TAU NEUTRINO, MUON, MUON NEUTRINO... HEAVY QUARK ORDER!?"

**Panel 5:**
God: "HA HA HA HA HA"
God: "I CAN'T WAIT TO SEE THE LOOK ON THEIR FACES."

**Panel 6:**
Caption: "...earth, 15 billion years later..."
Equations: "π⁻ → μ⁻ + ν̄μ" and "π⁻ → μ⁻ + ν̄μ"
Human 1: "?"
Human 2: "UTFP?"
Label: "INVOICE"

# Visual Note
The humor depends on the visual contrast between God's gleeful laughter at a billing mistake and the frustrated confusion of humans billions of years later, unable to solve the resulting particle physics equations.

comics.title: Life Imitates Art
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A multi-panel webcomic in black and white line art featuring stick figures representing physicists in an LHC control room. A crisis unfolds when a stable micro black hole forms, and the scientists unexpectedly turn to actor Wil Wheaton for help.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"DESPITE ASSURANCES FROM PHYSICISTS AROUND THE WORLD, THE UNTHINKABLE HAS HAPPENED. EARLIER TODAY, THE LARGE HADRON COLLIDER IN SWITZERLAND HAS CREATED A STABLE MICRO BLACK HOLE. THE SITUATION IS GROWING. NOW IT'S THREATENED TO DESTROY THE ENTIRE LHC FACILITY... AND THEN...

EARTH!"

**Panel 2:**
"MEANWHILE, THE WORLD'S TOP MINDS GATHERED AT THE LHC IN AN ATTEMPT TO SOLVE THE PROBLEM."

**Panel 3:**
"ACCORDING TO OUR DATA, THE BLACK HOLE WILL BEGIN TO EXPAND EXPONENTIALLY IN 47 SECONDS. THEN NOTHING CAN STOP IT."

**Panel 4:**
"I THINK I CAN HELP.

WIL WHEATON?!"

**Panel 5:**
"IS THIS SOME KIND OF JOKE, SON?

I'M WITH SAG. WE DON'T JOKE."

**Panel 6:**
"NOW THE WAY I SEE IT, THE BLACK HOLE IS FEEDING OFF OF A MATTER STREAM FROM A RESIDUAL PROTON CLOUD DRIFT IN THE CHAMBER."

**Panel 7:**
"THE FIRST THING YOU NEED TO DO IS FILL THE CAVITY WITH BARIUM. INJECT AN ELECTRON BUNCH AT EXACTLY THE RIGHT VELOCITY TO CREATE A PLASMA LIKE FIELD. ACCELERATION GRADIENT OF 1.2 GV/M. THAT SHOULD BE SUFFICIENT TO DISRUPT THE MATTER STREAM."

**Panel 8:**
"NO PROBLEM. I'LL JUST CALCULATE IT IN MY HEAD RIGHT NOW. I'LL ENTER THE DATA HERE... DONE!"

**Panel 9:**
"IT'S WORKING! THE BLACK HOLE IS BEGINNING TO EVAPORATE!"

**Panel 10:**
"YOU DID IT, WIL WHEATON! YOU SAVED THE PLANET WITH ONLY 3 SECONDS TO SPARE!"

**Panel 11:**
"AND HERE'S SOME COMPLIMENTARY COPIES OF MY BESTSELLING BOOK, 'THE HAPPIEST DAYS OF OUR LIVES'."

**Panel 12:**
"NOW YOU FINE LADIES AND GENTLEMEN CAN GET BACK TO YOUR HOLE GUEST OF UNLOCKING THE MYSTERIES OF THE UNIVERSE. HAPPY HIGGS BOSON HUNTING!"

**Panel 13:**
"AND REMEMBER - NEVER FORGET TO CHECK YOUR REFERENCES.

THANKS, WIL WHEATON!"

**Panel 14:**
"YOU SAVED US! YOU SAVED US ALL!"

**Panel 15:**
"*SIGH*"

**Panel 16:**
"THAT KID'S NOT SO BAD AFTER ALL."

**Panel 17:**
"MAKE NO MISTAKE ABOUT IT. WESLEY CRUSHER MAY HAVE SUCKED, BUT WIL WHEATON RULES!"

# Visual note

Wil Wheaton, an actor with no scientific background, casually solves an impossible physics problem in his head while the world's leading physicists cannot.

comics.title: Out of the Closet
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A stick-figure comic showing two characters in conversation across nine panels. The art style features basic circle heads and simple body outlines. The narrative progresses through escalating gang confrontation dialogue before deflating into an absurdist punchline.

# Text

**Panel 1 (caption):**
"...deep inside the secret hideout of the Dragons, the most feared gang in all of East L.A."

**Panel 1 (dialogue):**
"THE CORRA GANG BROKE INTO OUR GARAGE LAST NIGHT AND STOLE OUR BIKES."
"WHAT?!"

**Panel 2:**
"HOW MANY?"
"ALL OF THEM."

**Panel 3:**
"THAT'S THE LAST STRAW! THERE WILL BE BLOOD! I SAY WE POP TIRES IN ALL THEIR ASSES!"
"YEAH!" "YEAH!" "YEAH!"

**Panel 4:**
"NOBODY MESSES WITH THE F*CKING DRAGONS!"
"YEAH" "YEAH" "YEAH" "YEAH"

**Panel 5:**
"NOBODY INVADES OUR G*DDAMN TURF!"
"YEAH" "YEAH" "YEAH" "YEAH"

**Panel 6:**
"NOBODY TOUCHES OUR GORRAM BIKES!"

**Panels 7-8:**
(Silent)

**Panel 9:**
"OUR WHAT-BIKES?"
"NOTHING."

**Bottom (yellow fact box):**
"FACT: 1 out of every 10 people is a Browncoat"

# Visual Note

The humor relies on the escalating tough-guy rhetoric completely deflating when the narrator realizes they have been ranting about bikes they no longer possess.

comics.title: Calc-zilla
comics.comment: The union of the mathematician with the poet, fervor with measure, passion with correctness, this surely is the ideal. --William James, Collected Essays
comics.commentHtml: The union of the mathematician with the poet, fervor with measure, passion with correctness, this surely is the ideal. --William James, Collected Essays
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist stick-figure webcomic showing three simple smiley-faced characters and a monster with a large mouth. The comic progresses through nine panels depicting the characters being interrupted and attacked by the chaotic monster, with the narrative becoming increasingly absurd and violent.

# Text

**Panel 1:** "SO THE THREE HAPPY FRIENDS WERE JUST SITTING THERE, MINDING THEIR OWN BUSINESS..."

**Panel 2:** "...WHEN SUDDENLY... OH NO... HERE COMES THE ※ MONSTER !"

**Panel 3:** "OMG! HE'S EATING MR e³!"

**Panel 4:** "BUT WAIT... IT'S OK. HE'S JUST GOING TO POOP HIM RIGHT BACK OUT UNHARMED. THANK GOD."

**Panel 5:** "BUT OH NO, HERE HE COMES AGAIN! RUN! RUN AWAY! DAMNIT, IT'S TOO LATE. HE'S GOT ※ e³!"

**Panel 6:** "I CAN'T BEAR TO WATCH THIS. HE'S MAULING MR e² TO DEATH! SWEET FANCY MOSES!"

**Panel 7:** "THERE'S e³ BLOOD AND GUTS SPELLING ALL OVER THE PAGE! OH, THE HUMANITY!!!"

**Panel 8:** "RUN, MR y³, RUN! THERE'S NO TELLIN' WHAT THAT ※ BASTARD IS GONNA DO TO YOUR SORRY ASS..."
"OK, BILLY, THAT'S ENOUGH. SIT DOWN."

**Panel 9:** "BUT I'M NOT FINISHED YET."
"YES YOU ARE."
e^(x+e^y+x) = e^x+0+

# Visual note

The characters are labeled with mathematical notation (e³, e², y³). The monster consumes them literally, and the chaotic aftermath is rendered as mathematical symbols. An unfinished equation trails across the final panel as an adult interrupts a child's absurdist mathematical monster story.

comics.transcript:
# Scene
A six-panel black and white comic strip featuring two stick figures seated at a desk in an NSA office. A computer monitor is visible. The art style uses simple line drawings against a white background.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Caption: "...inside NSA headquarters. Room ___, OPS 1 Building"
Left figure: "WE INTERCEPTED THIS VIDEO WITH OUR KH-27 SATELLITE LAST NIGHT. IT HASN'T BEEN MADE PUBLIC YET."

**Panel 2:**
Right figure: "IF YOU LOOK AT TIME FRAME 23:10 TO 25:47, THE UPPER LEFT PIXELS FORM A PATTERN. IT'S DEFINITELY A SECRET MESSAGE."

**Panel 3:**
Left figure: "FOURIER ANALYSIS ON THE DEINTERLACED FRAMES REVEALED CIPHERTEXT ENCRYPTED WITH PGP 800-WD."

**Panel 4:**
Right figure: "DUAL_EC_DRGB? CAN WE USE THE BACKDOOR?"
Left figure: "NO GOOD. IT'S HOMEBREWED."

**Panel 5:**
Right figure: "HMMMM... DEVIOUS BASTARDS. WE'RE CURRENTLY BRUTE FORCING IT WITH OUR EXAFLOP SUPERCOMPUTER."

**Panel 6:**
Left figure: "IS IT WISE TO ALLOCATE SUCH AN IMPORTANT RESOURCE TO THIS ONE TASK?"

**Panel 7:**
Left figure: "IF THE RUSSIANS OR THE CHINESE DECODE THIS BEFORE WE DO, THE DIRECTOR WILL HAVE OUR ASSES."
Right figure: "GOOD POINT."

**Panel 8:**
Right figure: "YOU. THIS IS THE BEST EPISODE OF 'LOST'"
Left figure: "HELL, YEAH."

# Visual Note
The NSA agents deploy satellite surveillance, cryptographic analysis, and a supercomputer to decode what turns out to be an encrypted episode of the television show "LOST."

comics.title: Math vs Physics
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist comic strip featuring three characters—a blonde figure, a dark-haired figure, and a blue circle—in casual conversation. The art uses simple line drawings and geometric shapes.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Blonde character: "IF YOU WANT TO UNDERSTAND PHYSICS, YOU HAVE TO UNDERSTAND THE LANGUAGE IN WHICH SHE'S WRITTEN... MATHEMATICS."

**Panel 2:**
Blue circle character: "MATH IS JUST A TOOL INVENTED TO HELP US PHYSICISTS SOLVE PROBLEMS."

**Panel 3:**
Blue circle character: "YOU PHYSICISTS RELY TOO MUCH ON HEURISTIC REASONING."
Dark-haired character: "MATH HAS NO OBJECTIVE EXISTENCE."

**Panel 4:**
Dark-haired character: "ARE YOU SAYING WE'RE DELUSIONAL? ARE YOU SAYING WE'RE SLOPPY? ARE YOU SAYING WE'RE INFERIOR?"
Blue circle character: "SLAP"

**Panel 5:**
Blue circle character: "ARE YOU AS TURNED ON AS I AM?!"
Blue circle character: "*KISS* MORE!"

**Panel 6:**
Blonde character: "I GUESS WE CAN AGREE TO DISAGREE, BUT YOU MADE SOME GOOD POINTS."
Blue circle character: "THANKS."

**Panel 7:**
Blonde character: "WELL, IT'S GETTING LATE. I HAVE TO GET HOME."
Dark-haired character: "ME TOO."

**Panel 8:**
Blue circle character: "SHIT."

# Visual Note

The blue circle character and dark-haired character abruptly transition from intellectual debate into passionate kissing, only for both to leave moments later, leaving the blue circle character alone.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist stick-figure webcomic in black and white depicting an office worker at a computer. The first panel is in color. The comic shows the worker contemplating clicking an unfamiliar file, leading to increasingly chaotic consequences across subsequent panels.

# Text

Panel 2: "HMMM... NSFW. SHOULD I CLICK IT?"

Panel 3: "THE BOSS ISN'T HERE AND MOST OF MY COWORKERS ARE AT LUNCH."

Panel 4: "WHAT'S THE WORST THAT COULD HAPPEN?"

Panel 6: "*CLICK*"

Panel 8: "HELLO, NEO. DO YOU KNOW WHO I AM?" / "?!" / "MORPHEUS?"

Panel 9: "YES, THEY'RE COMING FOR YOU, NEO." / "WHO'S COMING FOR ME?"

Panel 10: "SEE FOR YOURSELF."

# Visual note

The comic parodies *The Matrix*. Clicking the NSFW file triggers the famous "Hello, Neo" dialogue with Morpheus, followed by multiple Agent Smith figures descending chaotically around the computer—a literal interpretation of Matrix narrative playing out on the office screen.

# Scene

A simple stick-figure webcomic showing a social gathering that progressively empties. The first three panels depict multiple characters in conversation; subsequent panels show an increasingly vacant space until only one figure remains.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Paris: "Will you be making the type 1 mistake?"
Other voices: "Are you preggers?" "Are you still friends?" "Paris" (repeated)

**Panel 2:**
Character: "Did you really just slap him?"
Another character: "Hey, look over there! It's Ed written!"

**Panel 3:**
Ed: "Will you be attending strings 2008?"
Ed: "What is the rest velocity of an unladen proton?"
Another character: "Are you still working on gauge theories in the Langlands?"

**Panels 4–5:**
(Silent)

**Panel 6:**
Know Stuff

# Visual note

The room empties progressively as Ed asks increasingly technical scientific questions about string theory, proton velocity, and gauge theories in the Langlands program, leaving only Ed remaining at the end.

# Scene

A minimalist stick-figure comic in a 3x3 grid. Two figures stand outside a door while a third figure peers out from inside. The art style is deliberately crude, emphasizing dialogue over visual detail.

# Text

Panel 1: (no text)

Panel 2: KNOCK KNOCK

Panel 3: 
- "CAN I HELP YOU GENTLEMEN?"
- "I THINK IT'S WE THAT CAN HELP YOU."

Panel 4:
- "HAVE YOU ACCEPTED THE SCIENTIFIC METHOD INTO YOUR HEART AND GIVEN YOUR LIFE OVER TO EMPIRICISM AND REASON?"
- "WHO ARE YOU GUYS?"

Panel 5:
- "WE JUST WANT YOU TO STOP LIVING IN THE DARKNESS OF IGNORANCE AND TO LET THE LIGHT OF SCIENCE ILLUMINATE YOUR UNDERSTANDING."

Panel 6:
- "LOOK, FELLAS, WE WERE JUST ABOUT TO START DINNER AND... WE ONLY NEED FIFTEEN MINUTES OF YOUR TIME."

Panel 7:
- "GET THE HELL OUTTA HERE!"
- "I'M SENSING HOSTILITY. WHY DOES THE TRUTH OFFEND YOU?"

Panel 8:
- "PERHAPS WE COULD JUST LEAVE YOU WITH A COPY OF NEWTON'S PRINCIPIA."

Panel 9:
- SLAM
- "THE TRUTH WILL SET YOU FREE!"
- "AMEN"

# Visual Note

The joke parodies door-to-door religious evangelism with science evangelists. The final panel's irony: despite rejecting them harshly, the figure inside responds "AMEN" to their departing statement, unconsciously accepting their proselytizing.

1907, Swiss Patent Office
F=ma
Σ

F=ma

IF EINSTEIN'S MIND WORKED ANYTHING LIKE MINE...

# Scene
Two stick-figure characters sit across from each other at a table. One has long dark hair, the other wears a blue baseball cap. The art style is minimalist with black lines on white background.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Character 1: "YOU KNOW, I'M REALLY JUST A GEEK INSIDE."

**Panel 2:**
Character 1: "I'M ALWAYS AFRAID TO MENTION IT ON THE FIRST DATE BECAUSE I DON'T WANT TO SCARE MY DATE AWAY."

Character 1: "I'M ACTUALLY HAPPY WHEN I HAVE HOURS OF FREE TIME TO JUST HACK AWAY ON SOME COMPUTER CODE."
Character 2: "REALLY? ME TOO!"

Character 1: "I'M EVEN HAPPIER WHEN I'M CONTEMPLATING THE MYSTERIES OF THE UNIVERSE WORKING ON UNSOLVED PROBLEMS IN THEORETICAL PHYSICS."
Character 2: "REALLY?! ME TOO!"

**Panel 5:**
Character 1: "AND I'M THE HAPPIEST WHEN I'M TRYING TO FORMULATE AN ELEGANT PROOF FOR SOME OPEN PROBLEM IN ABSTRACT MATHEMATICS."
Character 2: "REALLY? ME TOO!!"

**Panel 6:**
Character 1: "AND I ABSOLUTELY DON'T HAVE SEX UNTIL I'M MARRIED."
Character 2: "REALLY? ME T..."

**Panel 7:**
Character 1: "WAIT, WHAT?..."

**Panel 8:**
Character 2: "WHY NOT?"

**Panel 9:**
Character 1: "BECAUSE LIFE JUST ISN'T THAT GENEROUS YOU GREEDY BASTARD."

# Scene

A six-panel black and white stick figure comic featuring two round characters engaged in an escalating argument. The art style is minimalist line drawing with simple circular heads and basic features. The progression shows the characters becoming increasingly angry and physical, culminating in an unexpected resolution in the final panel.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Character 1: "I'M SICK OF ALL YER TALK. YOU'S FORCED WITH THE WRONG MAN."

**Panel 2:**
Character 1: "I'M GONNA MAKE YOU EAT THOSE WORDS, PUNK. I'M GOIN' GORDAN FREAKIN' ON YOUR ASS."
Character 2: "BRING IT."

**Panel 3:**
Character 2: "OH, I'LL BRING IT ALRIGHT. I'LL BRING THE HURT AND THE PAIN."
Character 1: "YOUR GRANDMA COULD BRING IT BETTER THAN YOU."

**Panel 4:**
Character 1: "YER GONNA LOOK AWFUL FUNNY WITH MY FOOT UP YO ASS. ENOUGH TALK, A**HOLE."

**Panel 5:**
Character 2: "LET'S GET IT ON!!!"

**Panel 6:**
(The two characters sit across from each other playing chess)

# Visual note

After five panels of escalating violent threats, the final panel reveals the characters have settled their dispute with a game of chess—a stark contrast to all the aggressive posturing.

# Scene

A minimalist stick-figure webcomic in black and white with simple line art. Two stick figures sit at a table—one bespectacled, the other with shoulder-length hair (later colored yellow). The emotional tone shifts from romantic to humorous and absurd.

# Text

Panel 2:
"HEY, WHAT ARE YOU DOING HERE ALL BY YOURSELF?"

"WELL THIS IS GONNA SOUND KINDA SILLY, BUT I MADE A PROMISE TO MYSELF."

Panel 4:
"I TOLD MYSELF THAT IF I EVER BUILT A TIME MACHINE, I WOULD GO BACK TO THE PAST AND MEET MYSELF RIGHT HERE... ON THIS DATE."

"IF EVERYTHING WORKS OUT, MY FUTURE SELF SHOULD BE ARRIVING RIGHT ABOUT... NOW."

Panel 6:
"THAT'S ABSURD. EVEN IF IT'S THEORETICALLY POSSIBLE FOR CLOSED TIMELIKE CURVES TO EXIST IN L..."

"?"

# Scene

A minimalist stick-figure webcomic featuring a woman with orange hair and a man with a blue circular head. The setting is simple line drawings, with a bowl of colorful candies as a central prop.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Woman: "I HEARD THAT YOU'RE NOT TALKING TO CHUCKY ANYMORE. I THOUGHT YOU GUYS WERE LIKE BEST FRIENDS SINCE FOREVER."

**Panel 2:**
Man: "WHAT HAPPENED?"

**Panel 4:**
Man: "HEY, WHATCHA DOIN'?"
Woman: "I'M ARRANGING MY M&MS BY COLOR."

**Panel 5:**
Man: "THEY HAVE TO BE EATEN IN ORDER OF INCREASING FREQUENCY IN THE ELECTROMAGNETIC SPECTRUM."

**Panel 6:**
Man: "FIRST, I EAT THE RED ONES. THEN I... DON'T BE RIDICULOUS. I WANT A BLUE ONE."

**Panel 7:**
Man: "NO!"

**Panel 8:**
"CHOMP CHOMP CHOMP"

**Panel 12:**
Woman: "NOTHING HAPPENED. I JUST DON'T LIKE HIM ANYMORE."

# Visual Note

The humor depends on the visual escalation: the absurd logic of eating candies in electromagnetic spectrum order, the man's escalating anger at the woman eating a blue candy out of sequence, and the implication that this trivial dispute ended their friendship.

comics.title: The Exception
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A nine-panel webcomic with two minimalist circular characters discussing a presentation. Each panel features a graph with colored lines and the equation En = -RT ln(x), with the characters shown in silhouette at the bottom of each panel.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"SO AS YOU CAN SEE, OUR RESEARCH RESULTS ARE VERY CLEAR."

**Panel 2:**
"ONCE AN INTERNET MEME EXCEEDS A CERTAIN THRESHOLD OF CRITICAL MASS, AWARENESS,..."

**Panel 3:**
"...IT PROPAGATES EXPONENTIALLY THROUGHOUT OUR CULTURE AND QUICKLY REACHES PSYCHOLOGICAL SATURATION."

**Panel 4:**
"AFTER THAT POINT, THE LEVEL OF AMUSEMENT DERIVED FROM THAT PARTICULAR MEME IS SIGNIFICANTLY DIMINISHED."

**Panel 5:**
"FOR EXAMPLE, THIS GREEN LINE REPRESENTS THE TRAJECTORY OF THE 'OM NOM NOM NOM' MEME."

**Panel 6:**
"EXCUSE ME, BUT WHAT IS THAT RED LINE?"

**Panel 7:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 8:**
"OH, THAT."

**Panel 9:**
"THAT'S CHUCK NORRIS, MOTHERFUCKER! UP HIGH."

# Visual Note

The red line continues rising indefinitely while the green line plateaus and declines, illustrating that Chuck Norris memes defy normal saturation patterns and remain perpetually popular.

# Scene
A stick-figure webcomic showing two characters sitting at a table across from each other in a simple interior. One character wears a blue hat. The comic progresses through nine panels depicting their conversation.

# Text

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:** "HEY, AREN'T YOU GOING TO B's PARTY? HE'S THROWING A KEGGER."

**Panel 3:** "I CAN'T GO OUT TONIGHT BECAUSE OF MY RULE." / "HUH?"

**Panel 4:** "EVERY NIGHT WHEN I GO TO SLEEP, I HAVE TO BE AT LEAST A LITTLE SMARTER THAN WHEN I WOKE UP. BUT I DIDN'T LEARN ANYTHING NEW YET TODAY."

**Panel 5:** "SO I FIGURE NOW IS A GOOD TIME TO TRY TO FINALLY GROW THE RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN DIOTIVIC COHOMOLOGY AND MINOR AT-THEORY. SO FAR, I'M..."

**Panel 6:** "DID YOU KNOW THAT THE SMALL PLASTIC SHEATHS ON THE END OF YOUR SHOELACES ARE CALLED AGLETS?"

**Panel 7:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 8:** "REALLY?"

**Panel 9:** "LOWER STANDARDS = MORE FUN"

# Visual Note
The final panel contrasts a solitary figure against a crowd of people celebrating, showing that accepting trivial facts as learning enables social participation rather than isolated self-improvement.

comics.title: So Many Questions
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist line drawing shows a small child sitting in a sandbox, wearing a blue cap. The child appears contemplative against a plain white background with black text and a yellow caption box.

# Text

DOES P=NP? WHAT HAPPENED BEFORE THE BIG BANG? ARE THERE LIMITS TO HUMAN KNOWLEDGE? WHAT IS MATHEMATICS, REALLY? WHAT IS GRAVITY, REALLY? WHAT IS SOY SAUCE, REALLY? IS PHYSICAL ACHIEVABILITY A PROPER SUBSET OF ABSTRACT CONCEIVABILITY? WHO SHOT JFK? WHO IS PIZZLER? WHO KILLED KENNEDY? WHY DID CAP'N CRUNCH TASTE SO MUCH BETTER WHEN I WAS 10? FOR CONVEX SURFACES OF FIXED INTRINSIC DIAMETER, IS THE DOUBLED DISK THE ONE WITH THE GREATEST AREA? DID GALORA THINK THAT THE UVVS WERE TOO UGLY TO SNUFF? IS THERE SUCH A STRONG STATISTICAL CORRELATION BETWEEN THE HOTNESS OF A WOMAN AND THE DOUCHEBAGGERY OF HER BOYFRIEND? IS MATHEMATICS INVENTED OR DISCOVERED? DOES SHE REALLY LIKE IT WHEN I DO THAT? CAN EVERY EVEN INTEGER GREATER THAN 2 BE WRITTEN AS THE SUM OF TWO PRIMES? WHY WON'T HEATHER EVER LET ME LOOK IN HER PURSE?...

Just some of the questions I would like answered before I kick

# Visual Note

The humor contrasts the child's innocent sandbox setting with an absurdist list mixing profound philosophical questions, personal grievances, and nonsensical non-sequiturs, culminating in a childish threat to "kick."

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist black-and-white comic set in an academic office. A student with a simple circular face approaches a professor of similar design sitting at a desk, presenting an assignment. The dialogue unfolds across nine panels.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Student: "HERE YOU GO, HERR PROFESSOR."
Professor: "?"

**Panel 2:**
Professor: "WHAT IS THIS?"
Student: "IT'S MY EXTRA CREDIT ASSIGNMENT."

**Panel 3:**
Professor: "THE LOCALLY EUCLIDEAN METRIZATION OF A K-FOLD CONTRAVARIANT RIEMANNIAN TENSOR FIELD BLAH BLAH BLAH..."

**Panel 4:**
Professor: "LOOK, FIRST OF ALL, I DIDN'T ASSIGN ANY CREDIT. SECOND OF ALL, THIS IS JUST A FRESHMAN CALCULUS COURSE."

**Panel 5:**
Professor: "THIRD, YOUR PERFECT EXAM SCORES ARE ALREADY SKEWING THE CURVE BEYOND ALL REASON."

**Panel 6:**
Student: "I DON'T WANT TO JUST SKEW THE CURVE. I WANT TO INCINERATE IT WITH THE WHITE HOT INTENSITY OF A THOUSAND SUNS."

**Panel 7:**
Professor: "'CRUSH YOUR ENEMIES, SEE THEM DRIVEN BEFORE YOU, HEAR THE LAMENTATIONS OF THEIR WOMEN!'"

**Panel 8:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 9:**
Student: "IT'S LIKE LOOKING IN A MIRROR."
Professor: "SNIFF"

# Visual Note

The humor depends on the student and professor having identical simple circular designs. When the student compares the moment to looking in a mirror, the visual similarity emphasizes that the professor sees his own ruthless ambition reflected in the student.

comics.title: The Most Popular Girl in School
comics.transcript:
# Scene
A minimalist black-and-white comic featuring a woman at a desk with a laptop and a man wearing glasses. The twelve panels depict a conversation between the two characters as the woman becomes increasingly flustered.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Equation on whiteboard: "∂u/∂t + Σ(j=1 to n) u_j ∂u/∂x_j = ∇△u - ∂ε/∂t + f(x)"
Subtext: "div u = Σ(j=1 to n) ∂u_j/∂x_j = 0"
Boundary condition: "u(x,0) = u°(x)"

**Panel 2:**
Woman: "EUREKA!"

**Panel 3:**
Man: "THERE'S A RUMOR GOING AROUND THAT YOU SOLVED THE NAVIER-STOKES PROBLEM. IS IT TRUE?"
Woman: "MAYBE. MAYBE NOT."

**Panel 4:**
Man: "IS THAT YOUR PROOF?"
Woman: "MAYBE. MAYBE NOT."

**Panel 5:**
Man: "CAN I SEE?"
Woman: "NO WAY!"

**Panel 6:**
Woman: "I WANT TO MAKE SURE THERE AREN'T ANY EMBARRASSING MISTAKES BEFORE I SHOW IT TO ANYONE."

**Panel 7:**
Man: "WELL, MAYBE WE COULD GO OUT FOR COFFEE OR SOMETHING. I'D LOVE TO PICK YOUR BRAIN."

**Panel 8:**
Woman: "HA HA. ONE LITTLE RUMOR AND SUDDENLY I'M THE MOST POPULAR GIRL IN GRAD SCHOOL."

**Panel 9:**
Woman: "TRUTH BE TOLD, I HAVEN'T EVEN DISCUSSED IT WITH MY THESIS ADVISOR YET AND..."

**Panel 10:**
[No dialogue]

**Panel 11:**
Man: "HEY, PAL, MY EYES ARE UP HERE. QUIT PEEKIN' AT MY EQUATIONS."
Woman: "I WASN'T PEEKING, I SWEAR."

**Panel 12:**
Woman: "LIAR!"
Man: "I WAS JUST STARING AT YOUR BREASTS."

# Visual note
The man's claim to have been looking at the woman's breasts is contradicted by the context: he was actually trying to read her mathematical proof over her shoulder.

# Scene

A stick figure comic with two characters: one with a blue circular head and one with a white circular head. The comic depicts a conversation where one character tells an increasingly absurd and gruesome story as a prank.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"...AFTER TAKING A SHOWER, SHE LIKES TO SWAB THE WATER OUT OF HER EARS WITH A Q-TIP."

**Panel 2:**
"ONE TIME SHE LEFT THE Q-TIP IN HER EAR AND JUST STARTED WALKING AROUND THE HOUSE."

**Panel 3:**
"SO SHE WAS STANDING IN THE KITCHEN MAKING A SANDWICH WITH THIS HUGE HONKIN' Q-TIP STICKING OUT OF HER EAR."

**Panel 4:**
"BUT SHE FORGOT IT WAS THERE AND LATER SHE FELT IT WIGGLING AROUND IN HER EAR AND SHE THOUGHT IT WAS A BUG."

**Panel 5:**
"SO SHE WENT TO SWAT IT..."

**Panel 6:**
"POW! JAMMED THAT BAD BOY RIGHT THROUGH HER FREAKIN' EARDRUM!"

**Panel 7:**
"OMG! IS SHE OK?"

**Panel 8:**
"OF COURSE SHE IS. I JUST TOTALLY MADE ALL THAT SHIT UP."
"WHAT?"

**Panel 9:**
"WHY THE HELL WOULD Y... TWO FOR CRINGING!"

# Visual Note

The punchline relies on the listener's visceral reaction to the fabricated story—the narrator scores points by making the listener cringe.

# Scene

A stick-figure webcomic depicting humanity's relationship with scientific discovery across five historical periods, from 212 BC to 2008 AD. Each panel shows the progression of scientific advancement alongside growing social resistance, drawn in simple black lines on white background with date labels.

# Text

**Panel 1 (212 BC):**
DO NOT DISTURB MY CIRCLES.
SHING

**Panel 2 (c. 1600 AD):**
OK, ABOUT THAT WHOLE EARTH-REVOLVING-AROUND-THE-SUN THING. MAYBE WE COULD JUST NOT TALK ABOUT IT.

**Panel 3 (1859 AD):**
I AM NOT A MONKEY'S COUSIN! GET THEE BEHIND ME, CHARLES! ...UR... I MEAN, SATAN!!!
*SIGH*

**Panel 4 (1895 AD):**
HEY! I COULD TRAVEL ALONG A BEAM OF LIGHT!
ALBERT! STOP DAYDREAMING IN CLASS! YOU'LL NEVER AMOUNT TO ANYTHING!!

**Panel 5 (2008 AD):**
SCIENCE
SHUT DOWN THE LABS!
EVOLUTION IS A LIE!
DOWN WITH THE SCIENTISTS
Y'ALL PISSIN' PEOPLE OFF, YOU AIN'T DOIN' IT RIGHT.

# Visual note

The humor contrasts crude depictions of major scientific breakthroughs with increasingly hostile societal reactions, showing resistance escalating from individual suppression to organized protest against science itself.

# Scene

A four-panel minimalist stick-figure comic set in a Large Hadron Collider control room. Two scientists discuss their search efforts, and a man in a hat appears in later panels. The comic uses black line drawings on a white background with a yellow highlight box containing text.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Caption: "MEANWHILE, AT THE LARGE HADRON COLLIDER..."
Figure 1: "ANY LUCK?"
Figure 2: "NADA."

**Panel 2:**
Figure 1: "WE'VE LITERALLY ANALYZED PETABYTES OF DATA. STILL NO SIGN OF THE HIGGS BOSON."

**Panel 3:**
Figure 2: "WELL... I'VE BEEN SEARCHING FOR THE HIGGS BOSON FOR 20 YEARS. I GUESS I'LL HAVE TO WAIT A LITTLE LONGER."

**Panel 4:**
Caption: "MR HIGGS SAYS HELLO!"
Sound effect: "ACKK"

**Panel 5:**
Sound effect: "GCRK"

**Panel 6:**
(Silent panel)

**Yellow highlight box:**
"You don't find the Higgs boson. The Higgs boson finds you."

# Visual Note

The punchline subverts the scientists' passive search by having the Higgs boson actively hunt them instead, using the structure of a famous action movie tagline.

comics.title: The Alpha Male
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A stick-figure comic in simple black and white line art depicting a conversation between two characters at a bar or table setting. One character wears glasses; the other does not. The interaction unfolds across eight panels, growing increasingly awkward.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"SO HERE'S MY NUMBER, GIVE ME A CALL SOME TIME."

**Panel 2:**
"GREAT, I'LL CALL YOU THIS WEEKEND."
"THIS WEEKEND? HMMMM... THAT'S A LITTLE TOO SOON."

**Panel 3:**
"I WAS THINKING OF... SAY... IN 5 YEARS."
"5 YEARS????"

**Panel 4:**
"LOOK, YOU SEEM LIKE YOU WOULD BE ABLE TO PROVIDE A STABLE NURTURING ENVIRONMENT FOR KIDS, BUT I WON'T NEED THAT UNTIL I'M IN MY 30s."

**Panel 5:**
*PASS SNACK*
"?"

**Panel 6:**
"C'MON, HURRY UP, TOOTS. MY HARLEY'S DOUBLE PARKED IN THE HANDICAPPED ZONE."

**Panel 7:**
"THANKS FOR THE DRINK."
"LATER, KID."
*CLICK*

**Panel 8:**
"OOH... SCORE."

# Visual Note

The character's true motivation is revealed through his actions and final comment—he was evaluating her as a potential mother for future children, not pursuing a romantic relationship.

comics.title: 936 Little Blobs
comics.comment: That last line is directed solely at my own damn self.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A grid of 936 small gray dots arranged left-to-right, top-to-bottom, representing months in a life. Scattered colored dots mark significant milestones. Below the grid is explanatory text and a legend identifying each colored dot's meaning.

# Text

LAST MONTH WENT BY PRETTY QUICKLY, DIDN'T IT? ONE MONTH IS NOT A VERY LONG TIME, BUT THE FACT OF THE MATTER IS THAT THE LIFE EXPECTANCY (IN THE UNITED STATES) IS ONLY ABOUT 936 MONTHS. LIFE SURE LOOKS DIFFERENT TO ME WHEN I CAN SEE IT OF MY LIFE DISPLAYED BEFORE ME AS 936 LITTLE BLOBS.

IF EACH BLOB REPRESENTS ONE MONTH OF YOUR LIFE TAKEN SEQUENTIALLY FROM LEFT-TO-RIGHT, TOP-TO-BOTTOM, HERE ARE SOME INTERESTING MILESTONES/STATS:

🔴 MOZART WRITES HIS FIRST SYMPHONY AT THIS AGE.
🔴 CONGRATULATIONS! YOU ARE NOW A WOMAN/MAN.
🟣 AVERAGE AGE OF WOMEN AT FIRST CHILD BIRTH.
🔵 EINSTEIN PUBLISHES PAPER ON SPECIAL RELATIVITY AT THIS AGE.
🟢 AVERAGE AGE OF FIRST MARRIAGE
🟠 AYN RAND PUBLISHES 'THE FOUNTAINHEAD' AT THIS AGE.
🔴 SORRY, YOU ARE NO LONGER ELIGIBLE FOR THE FIELDS MEDAL.
🟢 JOHN F. KENNEDY BECOMES 35th PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES AT THIS AGE.
🟡 AVERAGE AGE OF FIRST TIME GRANDPARENT
🔵 YOU MADE IT, MANY OF YOU ARE NOW ELIGIBLE FOR SOCIAL SECURITY BENEFITS AT THIS AGE.
⭕ YOU'RE ON BORROWED TIME NOW.

NOTE: WHERE APPROPRIATE, THE STATISTICS PERTAIN TO THE POPULATION OF THE UNITED STATES, BUT THE NUMBERS ARE VERY SIMILAR IN MANY OTHER PARTS OF THE WORLD.

NOW GET OFF YOUR ASS AND DO SOMETHING THIS MONTH!

# Visual Note

The grid of 936 dots makes abstract time concrete and finite, creating a visual representation of mortality. The scattered colored milestones emphasize how few months contain major life achievements.

comics.transcript:
# Scene
A stick figure sits at a computer, engaging in an increasingly frustrating conversation displayed in speech bubbles. The figure's frustration escalates as the responses become more absurd and repetitive.

# Text

**Title:** ARE YOU A KLINGON OR A COMPUTER?

1. "A WARRIOR DOES NOT ASK SUCH QUESTIONS."
2. "JUST ANSWER THE QUESTION."
3. "YOU HAVE NO HONOR!"
4. "WHY WON'T YOU ANSWER THE QUESTION?"
5. "IF YOU WERE ANY OTHER MAN, I'D KILL YOU WHERE YOU STAND!"
6. "OK, OK... SO WHAT DO YOU DO FOR FUN?"
7. "I TRAVEL THE RIVER OF BLOOD!"
8. "NO, SERIOUSLY, WHAT ARE YOUR HOBBIES?"
9. "I CRAVE ONLY THE BLOOD OF THE ENEMY!"
10. "DUDE, WHY ARE YOU SO ANGRY?"
11. "RAAAWR!!!!"
12. "WHY ARE YOU YELLING AT ME?"
13. "RAAAWR!!!!"
14. "I DON'T WANT TO PLAY THIS GAME ANY MORE."
15. "RAAAWR!!!!"

**Caption:** THE FIRST SUCCESSFUL KLINGON TURING TEST

# Visual note
The comic inverts the concept of the Turing Test—the human attempts to determine whether they're conversing with a Klingon or a computer program, but receives such consistently extreme warrior-like responses that the test succeeds through the human's surrender rather than definitive identification.

comics.title: Fun with Statistics
comics.transcript:
# Scene
A minimalist stick-figure comic with simple black line drawings on white backgrounds. A stick figure bounces on a trampoline, then is struck by lightning and crushed by a falling piano.

# Text
Panel 3: "I SPIT IN THE FACE OF DEATH!"

Panel 4: "ZAP"

Panel 6: "FACT: YOU HAVE A 39% GREATER CHANCE OF BEING STRUCK BY LIGHTNING AND THEN BEING EATEN BY A SHARK THAN YOU DO OF BEING KILLED BY A FALLING PIANO..."

# Visual note
The stick figure's confident declaration while bouncing on a trampoline is immediately contradicted when they are struck by lightning and then crushed by a falling piano—the exact outcome the statistics claim is statistically unlikely.

comics.title: Family Reunion
comics.comment: Inspired by true events.
comics.commentHtml: Inspired by true events.
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A nine-panel black and white webcomic showing three simplified figures seated at a table with food. One character has a striped circular head, one has a blue circular head, and one has black hair. The comic uses minimal line art to depict a family meal prayer that becomes increasingly graphic.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"DEAR LORD, WE THANK YOU THAT WE CAN COME TOGETHER AS A FAMILY EVERY YEAR FOR FOOD, FELLOWSHIP, AND FUN."

**Panel 2:**
"AND WE THANK YOU FOR GIVING US HUMANS DOMINION OVER THE ANIMALS."

**Panel 3:**
"THIS ALLOWS US TO RAISE COWS IN SQUALID LIVING CONDITIONS UNTIL WE SLAUGHTER THEM."

**Panel 4:**
"WE DO THIS BY APPLYING A 300 VOLT 2 AMP ELECTRIC SHOCK TO THE BACK OF THE COW'S HEAD TO KNOCK IT OUT."

**Panel 5:**
"AND THEN WE HANG IT FROM ITS HIND LEG WHILE ITS MAIN ARTERIES ARE SEVERED, ALLOWING ITS BLOOD TO DRAIN."

**Panel 6:**
"BUT I HEARD THAT ABOUT 30% OF THE COWS ARE NOT RENDERED FULLY UNCONSCIOUS BEFORE BEING STRUNG UP AND SO UNCEREMONIOUSLY EXSANGUINATED."

**Panel 7:**
"CONSIDERING THAT COWS HAVE A FAIRLY HIGH DEVELOPED NERVOUS SYSTEM, I CAN'T HELP BUT CONCLUDE THAT THAT SORT OF WRYTHING IN AGONY WOULD BE TOTALLY FUCKED UP IF YOU DID SOMETHING LIKE THAT TO CONSIDERING WHAT WE DO TO THEM."

**Panel 8:**
"SO THANK YOU, LORD, FOR NOT GIVING COWS ANY SOULS... CUZ SHIT, MAN... THAT WOULD BE TOTALLY FUCKED UP IF YOU DID."
"IN JESUS' NAME, AMEN."

**Panel 9:**
"AND I WAS NEVER ASKED TO SAY GRACE AT FAMILY REUNIONS AGAIN."
"... EVER."

# Visual Note

The humor contrasts a formal prayer structure with an increasingly graphic and profane description of industrial slaughter, culminating in the other characters' discomfort and the narrator's social consequence.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A man in a blue shirt sits on a stool speaking with three circle-headed figures. The comic is arranged in a 3x3 grid with minimalist line-drawn art.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"This is what I heard."
"GOOD SIR, I FIND IT QUITE INEXPLICABLE."

**Panel 2:**
"WHY DO YOU INSIST ON VIEWING TV DRAMAS INVOLVING CEREBRAL PLOT LINES INVOLVING THE IMAGINING OF PROBABLE FUTURE WORLDS?..."

**Panel 3:**
"WORLDS BASED ON LOGICAL EXTRAPOLATIONS OF CURRENT SCIENTIFIC AND CULTURAL TRENDS? I BELIEVE THIS TO BE INDICATIVE OF LATENT HOMOSEXUAL TENDENCIES..."

**Panel 4:**
"...WHICH, BY IMPLICATION OF MY VOCAL INTONATION, I DEEM TO BE A SOCIALLY UNACCEPTABLE LIFE-STYLE CHOICE IF ACTED UPON."

**Panel 5:**
"ME? I MUCH PREFER WATCHING A GROUP OF BUFF MEN IN TIGHTS ENGAGING IN A MASSIVE HOMOEROTIC DOGGIE PILE"

**Panel 6:**
"...ALL WHILE IN THE COMPANY OF MY SLIGHTLY-INEBRIATED, MALE-ONLY FRIENDS."

**Panel 7:**
"IN FACT, I HAVE AN INSATIABLE URGE TO DO SO RIGHT NOW..."

**Panel 8:**
"SO, WITHOUT FURTHER ADO, I TAKE MY LEAVE OF YOU, GOOD SIR."

**Panel 9:**
"HA HA HA HA"

**Panel 10:**
"But I'm pretty sure *this* is what he said."
"DUDE, WHY ARE YOU WATCHING STAR TREK? THAT'S SO GAY!"

**Panel 11:**
"C'MON, GUYS. LET'S GO WATCH SOME FOOTBALL."

**Panel 12:**
"HA HA HA HA"

# Visual Note

The comic contrasts the man's interpretation of crude mockery as elaborate formal language with the actual casual insult. The punchline reveals the irony that the speaker's preferred activity—watching football with male friends—is equally homoerotic as the activity he mocks.

# Webcomic Transcription

**Scene:** Two stick figures progress through life stages—childhood, education, careers, relationships—via parallel paths marked by arrows, eventually converging at hospital beds in old age.

**Text:**

"GOO." / "GAH."

"GAH." / "GOO."

"COLLEGE"

"DUDE, THAT SUCKED."

"DUDE, THAT SUCKED."

"YEAH, TOTALLY BLEW."

**Visual note:** The comic opens with two babies exchanging simple sounds ("GOO" and "GAH") and closes with the same two characters as elderly patients in hospital beds, now expressing life regret in nearly identical language. The cyclical structure creates a commentary on life's futility.

# Scene: A Transcript

Scene: A six-panel minimalist stick figure comic set in a bar. Three characters—a bald man, another man, and a long-haired woman—are present throughout.

Text: "SO AN ENGINEER, A PHYSICIST, AND A MATHEMATICIAN WALK INTO A BAR..." / QUAFF / QUAFF / QUAFF / BRAINS! / BRAIINNS / BRAIINNS / BRAIINNS / That is all.

Visual note: The comic subverts the classic joke setup by abandoning the expected punchline. Instead of wordplay or professional stereotypes, the three characters simply drink and then transform into zombies chanting "BRAINS!"—a literal and absurdist interpretation that mocks both the joke format and zombie tropes.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A black-and-white stick figure webcomic set on stairs. A scientist in yellow stands before a group of superheroes seated on the steps. The conversation reveals an ironic contrast between the heroes' flashy powers and the scientist's actual contributions to humanity.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
Scientist: "THANK YOU BOTH FOR APPLYING TO THE LEAGUE OF SUPERCOOL HEROES."
Batman: "UNFORTUNATELY, WE ONLY HAVE ROOM FOR ONE MORE MEMBER. SO LET'S START WITH YOU, SCIENTIST! DO YOU HAVE ANY COOL POWERS?"

**Panel 2:**
Scientist: "WELL, I'M A SCIENTIST. SCIENCE IS ALL ABOUT INCREASING HUMAN KNOWLEDGE... AND KNOWLEDGE IS POWER."
Batman: "YEAH, BUT, LIKE, ...CAN YOU FLY OR ANYTHING?"

**Panel 3:**
Scientist: "I HELPED DEVELOP THE PRINCIPLES OF AERODYNAMICS WHICH ALLOWED US TO MASTER WINGED FLIGHT."
Superman: "WE DISCOVERED THE PRINCIPLES OF GRAVITY AND MOTION THAT ALLOWED US TO LAND ON THE MOON."

**Panel 4:**
Batman: "BUT HAVE YOU SAVED ANY DAMSELS IN DISTRESS LATELY?"
Superman: "IT WAS THROUGH SCIENCE THAT WE DISCOVERED PENICILLIN, THE POLIO AND SMALLPOX VACCINES, AND MANY OTHER MEDICAL ADVANCES."

**Panel 5:**
Batman: "AND WITH THE ADVENT OF NEW TECHNOLOGIES SUCH AS GENE THERAPY, RNAI THERAPY, AND SYNTHETIC BIOLOGY..."
Superman: "...WE MAY SOON ERADICATE MANY OF THE MOST COMMON DISEASES KNOWN TODAY."

**Panel 6:**
Scientist: "SO, YES, IN FACT I HAVE SAVED MILLIONS OF LIVES."
Batman: "IN FACT, I BELIEVE THAT THROUGH THE POWER OF SCIENCE WE WILL ONE DAY ELIMINATE POVERTY, DISEASE AND POSSIBLY EVEN WAR."

**Panel 7:**
Scientist: "SO YES, THROUGH SCIENCE, WE WILL ALLEVIATE MUCH OF THE SUFFERING IN THE WORLD AND SAVE BILLIONS OF LIVES."

**Panel 8:**
Scientist: "OK,... WHAT ABOUT YOU, AQUAMAN? DO YOU HAVE ANY COOL POWERS?"
Aquaman: "I CAN TALK TO FISH."

**Panel 9:**
[Silent panel: all superheroes look embarrassed; Aquaman looks dejected]

# Visual note

The scientist's documented achievements in saving millions of lives through scientific advancement contrast sharply with Aquaman's useless superpower, underscoring the absurdity of the heroes' earlier dismissal of the scientist for lacking flashy abilities.

# Scene
Two stick-figure characters with circular heads discuss a mathematical discovery across nine panels. The art style is simple black and white line drawings.

# Text
**Panel 1:**

**Panel 3:**
Black character: "THIS IS GONNA TOTALLY BAKE YOUR NOODLE. THIS IS GONNA..."

"10² + 11²"

**Panels 5-9:**
"10² + 11² + 12² = 13² + 14²"

**Panel 9:**
Blue character: "HOLY SHIT."

# Visual note
The humor contrasts grandiose mathematical language about complex geometry with a simple integer equation, creating an anticlimactic reveal. The repeated "this is gonna..." phrase emphasizes the false promise of mind-blowing complexity.

# Scene

A six-panel comic strip in simple black-and-white line art. A round-headed character speaks in profile against a plain background with vertical lines suggesting a wall.

# Text

Panel 1: "...NOW NOTICE THAT YOU CAN'T UNTWIST THE BELT AFTER A 2π ROTATION."

Panel 2: "BUT IF YOU ROTATE IT BY 4π, THE BELT UNTWISTS."

Panel 3: "SO AS YOU CAN SEE, YOU CAN MODEL A SPINORIAL OBJECT WITH A BELT AND A BOOK."

The humor comes from the abrupt tonal shift. The character explains a genuine mathematical concept about spinors and topology before suddenly and rudely ejecting the audience, subverting expectations of a friendly conclusion.

comics.title: True Things
comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription: "Things I Know Are True (But Don't Really Believe)"

**Scene:**
A single-panel educational webcomic in black ink on white background featuring a stick-figure character presenting various scientific and mathematical concepts through diagrams, equations, and brief scenarios. The minimalist art style uses simple line drawings and mathematical notations.

**Text:**

"There are many things that I know are true ..."

QUANTUM ENTANGLEMENT

"IF A PAIR CREATES TWO ENTANGLED PHOTONS AND I MEASURE ONE..."

"...INSTANTANEOUSLY THE OTHER 'KNOWS' ABOUT MY MEASUREMENT ACROSS ANY DISTANCE"

"EINSTEIN 'DIDN'T LIKE IT' EITHER"

"BUT THE MATH WORKS OUT"

PACKET SWITCHING

"The internet is largely a packet switched network. The contents of this web page you are viewing was transported through the tubes as separate little packets (possibly) out of sequence and then reassembled before being displayed."

"INCONCEIVABLE!"

(EXTREME) INTERSPECIES VARIATION

"Drosophila Olfactory, Yao Ming, and I are the same species"

"NOT DRAWN TO SCALE BUT A ROUGH APPROXIMATION"

"Gisele Bündchen and I are the same species."

"YOU KNOW, TECHNICALLY, YOU AND I COULD MAKE A FERTILE OFFSPRING"

"NO."

THE WELL-ORDERING THEOREM

"WELL THEN, EVERY SET CAN BE WELL-ORDERED ON SET?"

"WHY NOT?"

"NO FUCK YOU"

NONEXISTENCE

"There once was a time when I did not exist... and there will be a time when I will not exist again."

"~13.7 BILLION YEARS AGO (BIG BANG)"

"PRESENT DAY"

"~10^100 YEARS FROM NOW (HEAT DEATH OF UNIVERSE)"

AERODYNAMICS

"Every 300 million people sit in a small metal tube 35,000 feet in the air traveling at 500 MPH eating honey roasted peanuts."

"P = ρ (1/2) v² ρ A + ..."

EULER'S IDENTITY

"e^(iπ) + 1 = 0"

"LIGHT THE HELL UP THIS IS BASICALLY SAYING THE FOUNDATION OF THE NATURAL WORLD IS BASED ON THE NATURAL LOGARITHM AND π"

RELATIVE SIZES

"Andromeda galaxy says the size of a grain of salt is visible the closest galaxy to us."

"If our solar system were the size of a grain of salt, the Milky Way would be 2 miles in diameter."

"If Earth were the size of a grain of salt, our solar system would be 2 football fields across."

"If a hydrogen atom was the size of a football field, its nucleus would be the size of a grain of salt."

"If the nucleus of a hydrogen atom was the size of a football field a neutron would be about the size of a grain of salt."

"If a hydrogen atom was the size of our solar system, a neutron would be about the size of an average star."

"... ≈ 3 × 10^(-7) × 10^(-42) × ..."

"INCONCEIVABLE! = E = √-1"

"... but I don't really believe."

A minimalist stick-figure comic in black and white depicting a person discovering a cat in a box and resolving to protect it.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
...1935
|ψ⟩ = ½|LIVE⟩ + ½|DEAD⟩/|CAT⟩

**Panel 2:**
MEOW

**Panel 3:**
[No text]

**Panel 4:**
[No text]

**Panel 5:**
MEOW

**Panel 6:**
I'LL NEVER PUT YOU IN DANGER AGAIN.
*SNIFF*

**Bottom banner:**
Happiness is a live cat.

# Visual Note

The comic references Schrödinger's cat, the 1935 quantum mechanics thought experiment. The punchline plays on the phrase "Happiness is a warm puppy" with "Happiness is a live cat."

# Webcomic Transcription

**Scene:** A black and white comic with nine panels showing stick figures representing subatomic particles as a vaudeville chorus line, progressing from a physics question to a theatrical performance.

**Text:**

Panel 1: "Physicists believe that they know what happened 10^-43 seconds after the big bang. But what happened before that?"

Panel 2: [Silent panel with black background]

Panel 3: "AZIZ, LIGHT!"

Panel 4: "BAME" [with spotlight effect]

Panel 5: "OH, WE'RE THE BOYS OF THE CHORUS"

Panel 6: "WE HOPE YOU LIKE OUR SHOW"

Panel 7: "WE KNOW YOU'RE ROOTING FOR US"

Panel 8: "BUT NOW WE HAVE TO GO!"

Panel 9: "We may never know."

**Visual note:** The particles perform as a chorus line before vanishing, conflating the unknowable origin of the universe with a theatrical performance that leaves without explanation.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A three-panel webcomic in simple black-and-white stick figure style. The first two panels show a stick figure at a desk with a time machine. The third panel is a large circular diagram with multiple stick figures positioned around it, trapped in a causal loop.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"I FINALLY FINISHED MY TIME MACHINE."

**Panel 2:**
"WHERE SHOULD I VISIT FIRST? MAYBE I'LL GO SEE SOME DINOSAURS."

**Panel 3 (Circular diagram, reading clockwise from top):**
"DON'T DO IT!"
"JUST COME BACK IN THE FUTURE TO WARN YOU."
"THE FUTURE ISN'T WHAT YOU WARNED ME ABOUT."
"WHY DIDN'T YOU WARN ME?"
"I TRIED TO WARN YOU ABOUT THIS."
"THERE IS NO PROBLEM!"
"KA-WHOOSH"
"GO BACK AND TELL YOURSELF NOT TO MAKE THE MACHINE."
"I BUILT IT BECAUSE OF YOUR WARNING."
"THE ONLY SOLUTION VISIT ANCIENT ROME TO SEE IF I DISSIPATE THERE."
"HUH?"
"NO PROBLEM."

# Visual note

The temporal paradox is depicted through a circular loop of figures and dialogue, where the character's attempt to prevent a problem by warning their past self creates the problem, trapping them in an endless causal loop.

A three-panel webcomic in simple stick-figure style. A character sits at a computer in minimalist black-and-white drawing, with occasional color accents.

**Panel 1 (Main):** ">>>"

**Panel 2:** ">>> import bacon"

**Panel 3 (Footer):** "...awesomeness."

# Visual note

The joke relies on a double meaning: the Python programming command `import` (which loads modules) is executed literally, causing bacon strips to appear on screen.

# Scene

A worksheet displaying 16 hand turkey drawings labeled A through P in a grid layout. Each drawing represents a different person's interpretation of a hand turkey, ranging from simple outlines to elaborate artistic renderings, crude sketches, and abstract variations.

# Text

**Can you guess who drew each of the following hand turkeys?**

assume a turkey

# Visual note

The humor depends on the extreme variation in artistic skill and interpretation across the drawings. Some are basic outlines, others are highly detailed with decorative elements, one is nearly blank, one is just a hand outline labeled "assume a turkey," and one consists of chaotic multicolored scribbles. The diversity suggests these were drawn by people with vastly different artistic abilities or approaches.

comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription

**Scene:**
A minimalist stick-figure comic progressing from a solitary figure on a hillside at night through scientific and technological imagery before returning to the original scene. The narrative uses black and white line drawings with occasional color accents.

**Text:**

"IS IT POSSIBLE TO READ THE MIND OF GOD?"

"GOD, IF YOU'RE THERE, SHOW ME A SIGN."

"WOW! WHAT A COINCIDENCE"

"Is it possible to read the mind of God?

We have been asking that question since the dawn of human history. It is this inquisitive nature that defines us. It is this inquisitive nature that propels and compels us. It compels us to try to understand the universe.

It compelled our ancestors to contemplate the mysteries of mathematics.
ΑΙΤΙΟΜΑΤ-
ΜΗΔΕΙΣ ΕΙΣΙΤΩ

It compelled us to build soaring monuments with remarkable feats of engineering.

It compelled us to calculate the motion of heavenly bodies.

...and to unlock Nature's most stubborn secrets.

Today, that same curiosity propels the frontier of our knowledge and technology.

Every day, we get better at harnessing more energy and manipulating it to our bidding.

...while, at the same time, we're getting better at manipulating more with less energy.

How long will it be before we are compelled to merge with our technology, mind & body?

Will there ever be a time when we can no longer distinguish ourselves from our technology?

...or individuals from each other?

Then as we infuse the world around us with more of our technology, aren't we, in fact, infusing it with ourselves?

Surely this infusion of our intelligence will expand throughout our solar system, and beyond.

When we reach that point where we can manipulate all of the energy and matter in the universe,

...when we can manipulate space and time to do our bidding,...

...will we, then, finally be able to read the mind of God?

Perhaps we never had a"

# Scene

A vertical flowchart-style comic in simple black and white line art depicting the journey of oil from extraction to modern life. The art shows the geological and industrial process of fossil fuels, culminating in two small dinosaurs at the bottom observing the consequences.

# Text

WEEEEEEE!!!

GAS

I DUNNO, CARL. I JUST THOUGHT IT WAS SOME KINDA NEAT GLOWING POWDER IN THE SKY.

# Visual note

The comic traces oil backwards from a car driving away through gas stations, oil rigs, and underground geological layers to fossilized dinosaur remains, finally revealing the two conversing dinosaurs are the ancestors of the fuel being used. The dinosaurs are completely unaware of their ultimate fate.

Two people lie in bed together. One has dark hair, the other wears a blue baseball cap. The art style is minimalist black and white sketch.

# Text

**Person with dark hair:**
"I'M SORRY, I THOUGHT GUYS LIKED IT WHEN WE DID... THAT."

**Person in blue cap:**
"WE DO... BUT..."

**Person in blue cap (thought bubble):**
"...BUT WHERE DID SHE LEARN HOW TO DO THAT AND HOW DID SHE GET SO GOOD AT IT? WHICH ONE OF HER DOUCHEBAG EX-BOYFRIENDS TAUGHT HER THAT? WAS IT CHIP, THAT GUY WITH THE BARBED WIRE TATTOO?...OR WAS IT JAKE, THE SEMI-PRO FOOTBALL PLAYER WITH THE MOHAWK? MAYBE SHE JUST WATCHES A LOT OF PORN; OR MAYBE..."

# Visual Note
The humor contrasts the man's polite external response with his spiraling internal monologue of jealous speculation about his partner's sexual history and skills.

# Scene

A multi-panel comic in simple stick-figure style depicts an escalating disaster scenario, progressing from a power station control room to a map of the United States to a global view, showing the cascading consequences of a decision made by workers.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
POWER STATION
Person 1: "OVERLOAD! OVERLOAD! SHUT IT DOWN!"
Person 2: "BUT THAT WILL BLACK OUT THE ENTIRE EASTERN SEABOARD."
Person 3: "DO IT!"

**Panel 2:**
Map of USA with eastern region colored black
Caption: "PROBABLE EMP ATTACK. LAUNCHING COUNTERATTACK."
Label on equipment: "DEFENSE DEFENSE WOPR"

**Panel 3:**
Image of Earth with multiple explosion clouds surrounding it

# Visual Note

The humor derives from a chain-reaction escalation: shutting down the power station to prevent overload inadvertently triggers a military defense system's automated response to the blackout, which misinterprets it as an EMP attack and launches a counterattack, resulting in global nuclear warfare.

# Webcomic Transcription

**Scene:** A minimalist stick figure comic featuring two characters—one wearing a blue helmet and one with black hair—in a 3×3 grid of panels on a white background. The characters repeatedly pass through each other's bodies in identical poses.

**Text:** 
"This happens to me about once a week."

"OMG, I THINK I FINALLY UNDERSTAND QUANTUM MECHANICS."
"REALLY?"

"NO, WAIT. IT PASSED."

**Visual note:** The humor depends on the visual repetition of the two figures interpenetrating across multiple panels. One character achieves sudden understanding of quantum mechanics while experiencing this surreal phenomenon, but loses the insight immediately once it stops occurring.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist stick-figure webcomic depicting a character's Christmas Eve walk through their neighborhood. The narrative progresses from quiet melancholy to defiant emotional catharsis, using simple black line drawings with occasional yellow accents.

# Text

Panel 1: "I have an annual holiday tradition."

Panel 2: "Every Christmas, at night..."

Panel 3: "...I take a walk around my neighborhood alone."

Panel 4: "It's the loneliest night of the year."

Panel 5: "The streets are never emptier than on Christmas night."

Panel 6: "Sometimes, I peer into the windows of the houses I pass."

Panel 7: "Everybody seems so happy on this special night, surrounded by the warmth of family and friends."

Panel 8: "...as if the universe refuses to acknowledge my existence."

Panel 9: "One time, I saw a young couple walking the streets, too,..."

Panel 10: "...lost in their own cocoon of happiness and joy, completely oblivious to my presence."

Panel 11: "It makes me feel... insignificant."

Panel 12: "ACKNOWLEDGE MY EXISTENCE! / ACKNOWLEDGE MY EXISTENCE!"

Panel 13: "I EXIST, DAD GAMMI!!!"

Panel 14: "I may be insignificant..."

Panel 15: "MERRY F*CKING CHRISTMAS!"

Panel 16: "...but the universe will NOT ignore me."

# Visual Note

The character's emotional state shifts from quiet desperation to explosive, frantic defiance, with increasingly erratic gestures and profanity marking their cathartic moment.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
EFFECT ON EVIL PLAN
KE = ½(6.16×10^24 kg)(1.21×10^5 m/s)²
= 3.7×10^34 J
P = 1.2 × 10^25 W

**Panel 2:**
DARK FORCE (ENERGY)
S = ∫d⁴x√-g[¹⁄₁₆πR - ½gᵘᵛ∂ᵤφ∂ᵥφ - V(φ)]
∂ + ½∂̄∂ + ³⁄₈ = 0, V= g²/φ
P = 4π·7×10^25 W
EFFECT ON EVIL PLAN = ∞

**Panel 3:**
"YOU'RE RIGHT, LORD VADER, THE ABILITY TO DESTROY A PLANET IS NOT STATISTICALLY SIGNIFICANT COMPARED TO THE POWER OF THE FORCE."

# Visual note
The humor derives from rigorous physics calculations showing that the Death Star's destructive power is negligible compared to the theoretical energy of "the Force," making its planet-destroying capability statistically insignificant.

# Scene

A stick figure webcomic in black and white. A robber with a gun confronts victims in an escalating series of exchanges that parody crime film dialogue while centering on an obscure mathematical concept.

# Text

**Panel 1:** (Silent)

**Panel 2:** 
Robber: "STICK 'EM UP!"

**Panel 3:**
Victim: "PLEASE DON'T SHOOT, JUST TAKE MY WALLET!"
Robber: "RELAX. I DON'T WANT YOUR MONEY."

**Panel 4:**
Robber: "I WANT INFORMATION."

**Panel 5:**
Robber: "WHAT DOES THE FISCHER-GRIESS MONSTER LOOK LIKE?"
Victim: "WHAT?"

**Panel 6:** (Silent)

**Panel 7:**
Victim: "WHAT COUNTRY ARE YOU FROM?"
Robber: "WHAT?"

**Panel 8:**
Victim: "'WHAT' AIN'T NO COUNTRY I EVER HEARD OF. THEY SPEAK ENGLISH IN 'WHAT'?"
Robber: "WHAT?"

**Panel 9:**
Robber: "ENGLISH, MOTHERFUCKER! DO YOU SPEAK IT?!"
Victim: "YES!"

**Panel 10:**
Robber: "THEN YOU KNOW WHAT I'M SAYING. DESCRIBE THE FISCHER-GRIESS MONSTER!"
Victim: "WHAT?"

**Panel 11:**
Robber: "SAY 'WHAT' AGAIN! I DARE YOU! I DOUBLE-DARE YOU, MOTHERFUCKER! SAY 'WHAT' ONE MORE GODDAMN TIME!"

**Panel 12:**
Victim: "I-I-IT'S A FINITE GROUP."
Robber: "GO ON!"

**Panel 13:**
Victim: "IT'S THE LARGEST OF THE SPORADIC GROUPS."
Robber: "DOES IT LOOK LIKE A BITCH?"

**Panel 14:**
Victim: "WHAT?"
(Robber shoots: "BANG")

**Panel 15:**
Victim: "OOOOH"
(Robber walks away)

**Panel 16:**
Victim (on phone): "911 EMERGENCY. HELP, I'VE BEEN SHOT."

**Panel 17:**
Dispatcher: "WE'LL SEND HELP RIGHT AWAY, BUT FIRST, WHAT'S THE ORDER OF THE MONSTER GROUP?"
Victim: "WHAT? HOW SHOULD I KNOW?"

**Panel 18:**
Dispatcher: "I'M SORRY, SIR. THIS LINE IS RESERVED FOR PEOPLE WHO KNOW ELEMENTARY GROUP THEORY."
Victim: "WHAT?"
(*CLICK*)

**Panel 19:**
Dispatcher (to young child): "AND THAT IS THE REASON, SON."

**Panel 20:**
Parent: "THAT'S WHY YOU NEED TO LEARN MATH."

**Panel 21:**
Parent: "NOW GO TO YOUR ROOM AND DO YOUR HOMEWORK."

# Scene

A horizontal comic strip in simple stick-figure style depicting a New Year's countdown. Stick figures become increasingly compressed and distorted as time counts down, progressing from recognizable forms to vertical lines, culminating in a colorful celebration scene with the number 2009 and party elements.

# Text

"... 3, 2, 1, ..."
"... 2 ..."
"1"
"2009"
"MAY YOUR NEW YEAR CONVERGE TO INTERESTING TIMES."

As the countdown progresses, the stick figures compress and distort toward a single point, creating a visual metaphor for mathematical convergence that mirrors the caption's double meaning—both the convergence of time to midnight and convergence toward "interesting times."

comics.title: Computer Programming 101

comics.commentHtml: Thank God some people don't need to see so far under the hood.
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A six-panel webcomic in black and white stick-figure style showing two characters in conversation. One character asks questions while the other provides explanations. Yellow caption boxes mark time passages. The comic illustrates a recursive loop of increasingly fundamental concepts.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"...THEN YOU NEED TO INVOKE THE COMPILER AND..."
"WHAT'S A COMPILER?"

**Panel 2:**
"WELL,... IN THIS CASE, A COMPILER TRANSLATES SOURCE CODE INTO OBJECT CODE..."

**Panel 3:**
"...WHICH IS THEN PASSED TO A LINKER WHICH TURNS IT INTO MACHINE CODE."
"WAIT... WHAT'S MACHINE CODE?"

**Panel 4:**
"MACHINE CODE IS THE CODE THAT CAN BE DIRECTLY EXECUTED BY THE CPU OF THE..."
"WHAT'S A CPU?"

**Panel 5:**
"THE CPU IS A MICROPROCESSOR THAT..."
"WHAT'S A MICROPROCESSOR?"

**Panel 6:**
"10 minutes later..."
"...SO THE POSITIVE CHARGE ATTRACTS ELECTRONS FROM P-TYPE SILICON THAT SEPARATES THE..."
"WHAT'S AN ELECTRON?"

**Panel 7:**
"30 minutes later..."
"...SO ACCORDING TO THIS THEORY, SYMMETRY BREAKING OCCURS AT ABOUT 100 GeV..."
"WHAT'S A GeV?"

# Visual Note

Each explanation triggers a new "What is X?" question, creating a chain that descends from programming concepts through hardware to particle physics.

comics.title: Lie of Omission
comics.transcript:
# Scene: A minimalist stick-figure webcomic depicting a couple in bed. The male character wears a blue cap; the female character has black hair. The narrative follows their conversation through multiple scenes in their bedroom.

# Text

Panel 1: "HEY, WHAT'S ALL THIS?" / "I BRING PIZZA AND BEER. HAPPY 5 MONTH ANNIVERSARY."

Panel 2: "AND THESE ARE MY SORORITY SISTERS FROM COLLEGE AND MY OTHER LEADER GIRLFRIENDS FROM HIGH SCHOOL."

Panel 3: "I TOLD THEM HOW SPECTACULAR YOU WERE IN BED AND I SHARE EVERYTHING WITH MY GIRLFRIENDS."

Panel 4: "SO THEY'RE HERE TO RE-ENACT ALL OF YOUR ROLE-PLAYING SEXUAL FANTASIES."

Panel 5: "WOW, YOU SHOULDN'T HAVE." [WHISPERING, TORI!, LELIA]

Panel 6: "GO GET HIM, GIRLS!"

Panel 7: [MULTIPLE SEX LIGANDS WOOO HOOD!...]

Panel 8-10: [Wordless scenes]

Panel 11: "HEY, WHATCHA THINKIN' ABOUT?"

Panel 12: "I WAS JUST WONDERING WHAT IT WOULD BE LIKE IF ALL YOUR GIRLFRIENDS CAME OVER TO FULFILL ALL MY SEXUAL FANTASIES."

Panel 13: [Wordless scene]

Panel 14: "GET OUT, GET OUT OF THE HOUSE." / "WHAT?"

Panel 15: "BUT THIS IS MY HOUSE." / "GO TO A HOTEL."

Panel 16: "BUT..." / "GTFO!"

Panel 17: [Wordless scene]

Panel 18: "HEY, WHATCHA THINKIN' ABOUT?" / "YOU."

Panel 19: [Wordless scene]

Panel 20: "AWWW... HOW SWEET."

Panel 21: "YOU JUST EARNED YOURSELF A CUDDLE, MISTER."

Panel 22: "LET'S FORGET THE REST OF THE WORLD. NO TV, NO INTERNET, NO VIDEO GAMES."

Panel 23: "LET'S NEVER GET OUT OF BED AGAIN. NOT EVEN FOR THE EAGLES' POST SEASON GAMES."

Panel 24-25: [Wordless scenes]

Panel 26: "HEY, WHATCHA THINKIN' ABOUT?"

Panel 27: [Wordless scene]

Panel 28: "FOOTBALL." / "HA, MEN ARE SO SIMPLE MINDED."

# Visual note

The repeated question "Hey, whatcha thinkin' about?" drives the humor through contrasting answers: a self-centered fantasy (rejected), a romantic answer (rewarded), and finally football (revealing his actual priorities despite the intimate moment).

comics.title: The Pantheon
comics.comment: Getting rid of a delusion makes us wiser than getting hold of a truth but no man is happy without a delusion of some kind.
comics.commentHtml: Getting rid of a delusion makes us wiser than getting hold of a truth but no man is happy without a delusion of some kind.
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A black and white stick figure webcomic showing two long-haired characters in conversation within a pantheon setting. A third character wearing a blue hat interrupts their discussion. The final panel displays three portrait frames side by side.

# Text

Panel 1:
"GRÜB GOTT, ISAAC. WHAT IS THIS PLACE?"

Panel 2:
"MY DEAR ALBERT, I CAN ONLY ASSUME THAT WE ARE IN THE PANTHEON OF GREAT SCIENTISTS."

Panel 3:
"INDEED, WE ARE TWO OF THE GREATEST MINDS IN HISTORY."

Panel 4:
"IT IS DOUBTFUL THAT ANY OTHER MIND WILL EVER JOIN US IN THIS GREAT PANTHEON."

Panel 5:
"HEY, GUYS! WHATCHA DOIN'?"

Panel 6:
"URR... SAY, CHAP... ARE YOU CERTAIN YOU'RE IN THE RIGHT..."
"SHHH... SHUT UP, ISAAC."

Panel 7:
"LET'S JUST HUMOR HIM."

# Visual note

The humor contrasts the two historical figures' self-assured conversation about their exclusive pantheon with the sudden arrival of an unqualified third character in a blue hat, who is silently admitted despite their obvious unsuitability. In the final panel, the cartoon character's portrait hangs alongside the historical scientists' portraits.

comics.title: some advice...
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A stick-figure comic strip in simple black and white line art. A customer with a blue head orders food at a fast-food counter from a worker with a black head. The comic escalates from a simple order into an absurd infomercial-style rant that spirals out of control.

# Text

Panel 1:
Customer: "...AND A BIG MAC AND A SMALL COKE, PLEASE."

Panel 2:
Worker: "WHY?"
Customer: "HUH?"

Panel 3:
Worker: "WHY BUY A BURGER WHEN YOU CAN MAKE IT AT HOME?"

Panel 4:
Worker: "ALL YOU NEED IS..."

Panel 5:
Worker: "...THE MIGHTY BURGATRON 3000!"

Panel 6:
Customer: "HI, BILLY MAYS HERE!"

Panel 7:
Customer: "NO MORE SQUISHIN' AND SQUASHIN' THOSE BURGERS!... FORGET IT. I'M LEAVING."
Worker: "STOP FOLLOWING ME."

Panel 8:
Worker: "...OR FLIPPIN' AND FLOPPIN'!..."
Customer: "LEAVE ME ALONE."

Panel 9:
Worker: "JUST SCOOP, PRESS, AND COOK IN JUST 2 MINUTES!..."

Panel 10:
Worker: "IT'S FAST! IT'S EASY!"
Customer: "STOP YELLING AT ME."

Panel 11:
Worker: "HI, BILLY MAYS HERE FOR MIGHTY MENDIT!"

Panel 12:
Customer: "BEFORE YOU THROW IT AWAY, LET MIGHTY MEND IT SAVE THE DAY!"
Worker: "WTF?"

Panel 13:
Worker: "ORDER NOW AND WE'LL TRIPLE THE OFFER!!!"
Customer: "GO AWAY!"

Panel 14:
Worker: "HI, BILLY MAYS HERE!"

Panel 15:
Worker: "HI, BILLY MAYS HERE! BILLY MAYS HERE! BILLY MAYS HERE!"

Panel 16:
Worker: "HEY! OFFER YET—IT'S NOT QUANTITY & !!!...[unintelligible shouting]"

Panel 17:
Caption: "Never fall asleep with the television on."
(Customer sleeping in bed with TV on)

# Visual Note

The worker's character multiplies across panels as the Billy Mays persona takes over, visually representing an escalating, uncontrollable infomercial rant. The final panel reveals the entire sequence was a dream caused by falling asleep with the television on.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist stick figure comic showing a man instructing another man (presumably his son) on how to handle a marital situation. The scene takes place indoors and uses simple black and white line drawings.

# Text

Panel 1: "OK, LET'S TRY THIS AGAIN, SON."

Panel 2: "SAY YOU'RE JUST SITTING THERE WATCHING TV MINDING YOUR OWN BUSINESS..."

Panel 3: "WHEN ALLUVA SUDDEN, UH OH, HERE COMES YOUR WIFE..."

Panel 4: "...AND SHE ASKS, 'DOES THIS DRESS MAKE ME LOOK FAT?'"

Panel 5: "WHAT DO YOU SAY?"

Panel 6: (No dialogue)

Panel 7: "'WHY NO, DEAR, YOU LOOK BEAUTIFUL IN ANYTHING.'"

Panel 8: "GOOD. THAT'S GOOD, SON."

Panel 9: "BUT THEN SHE SAYS, 'YOU'RE NOT EVEN LOOKING AT ME. LOOK AT ME.'"

Panel 10: "'I AM LOOKING.'"

Panel 11: "'NO YOU'RE NOT; STOP LYING TO ME, LOOK AT ME, DAMMIT.' 'BUT...'"

Panel 12: "'LOOK IT ME, YOU WORTHLESS COUCH POTATO!' 'I'M LOOKING. I'M LOOKING.'"

Panel 13: "'HOW DID YOU EVER TRICK ME INTO THIS LOVELESS SHAM OF A MARRIAGE?!' 'ANSWER ME! I DON'T WANNA PLAY ANYMORE...'"

# Visual note

The humor escalates through successive panels as the scenario spirals from a simple compliment into an absurd marital argument, illustrating the futility of the "lesson" being taught.
