# Scene: A Civilization in Motion

A black and white line-drawn illustration depicts a panoramic view of human civilization and activity across time and space. The composition shows multiple simultaneous scenes: volcanic eruptions and early human activity on the left, famous landmarks in the center background, modern cities and infrastructure in the center, and maritime activities on the right. The foreground shows crowds of people, animals, vehicles, and aircraft. The top features cosmic imagery including planets, moon phases, and stars.

# Text

"Time is what prevents everything from happening at once."
—John Archibald Wheeler

# Visual note

The illustration ironically contradicts the quote by depicting everything happening simultaneously in a single frame—past civilizations, present-day activities, various modes of transportation, natural disasters, space exploration, and human history all coexist together.

# Scene
A minimalist comic with two circular characters—one with a blue semicircular head and one plain white circle—depicting an escalating argument across multiple panels that culminates in the blue character alone, contemplating.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"ANYWAY, THAT'S MY OPINION ON THE SUBJECT."

**Panel 2:**
[Untranslatable characters/symbols]

**Panel 3:**
"WHAT? THAT'S A COMPLETE MISINTERPRETATION OF WHAT I SAID."

**Panel 4:**
[Untranslatable characters/symbols]

**Panel 5:**
"YOUR ARGUMENT IS UTTERLY DEVOID OF LOGIC."

**Panel 6:**
[Untranslatable characters/symbols]

**Panel 7:**
"THAT IS LITERALLY THE STUPIDEST THING I'VE EVER HEARD! AND IT'S A BIT RACIST."

**Panel 8:**
[Untranslatable characters/symbols]

**Panel 9:**
"WTF! ARE YOU TRYING TO OUT-STUPID YOURSELF?!"

**Panel 10:**
[Untranslatable characters/symbols]

**Panel 11:**
"I'M A KICK YOU IN YOUR ASS!!! WAIT, YOU'RE FORGETTING SOMETHING."

**Panel 12:**
"AND WHAT IS THAT? THIS ARGUMENT OCCURRED 3 YEARS AGO AND NOW IT ONLY EXISTS IN YOUR IMAGINATION."

**Panel 13:**
"I am my own worst enemy."

# Visual Note
The listener's responses are rendered as illegible symbols, forcing the reader to imagine increasingly absurd statements that escalate the speaker's frustration. The final reveal—that the entire argument exists only in the speaker's mind—provides the comic's punchline about ruminating over past conflicts.

comics.transcript:
# American History by Turkey

## Scene
A minimalist stick-figure comic with simple geometric backgrounds in shades of green, blue, and black. Six panels arranged in a grid show a narrative progression using stark black silhouettes and limited color palettes, concluding with a large turkey silhouette at the bottom.

## Text

**Panel 1:**
"Our land was a bountiful land filled with awe-inspiring beauty."

**Panel 2:**
"We roamed free over rolling green hills and pristine fields of grass."

**Panel 3:**
"And then THEY came. The giants. A most grotesque alien race from a far away place."

**Panel 4:**
"They occupied our land. They came like locusts with their hideous technology and terrible weapons. They arrived in ships so large it boggled the imagination. And they started to slaughter my people without mercy."

**Panel 5:**
"My people were herded into wretched prison camps where they could do nothing but wait to die."

**Panel 6:**
"But the most dreadful time is 'The Festival', the time of year when the aliens perform their ritual slaughter of our people by the millions. And before they consume our flesh, they perform a most sadistic ceremonial chant to their god."

**Bottom section:**
"Yet some of us still roam free beyond the power of the aliens. We few, we band of freedom fighters, will carry on the resistance. We will not vanish without a fight. We will not rest until *all* of our brothers and sisters can once again fly free."

**Footer:**
"AMERICAN HISTORY

by Turkey"

## Visual note
The comic is a satirical retelling of American colonial history from the turkey's perspective, with "the aliens" representing European colonizers, "The Festival" referring to Thanksgiving, and the turkey as a freedom fighter—ironic given that turkeys are killed during the holiday being described.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene
A minimalist line-drawing comic in nine panels, followed by a notebook page section. The panels show a figure using a telescope pointed at a nighttime cityscape and a window, with silhouettes visible inside. The final panel reveals Jupiter through the telescope.

## Text

**Panel 1:** "Ahhh... the city is so beautiful at night."

**Panel 2:** "Well, well, well... what have we here?"

**Panel 3:** "Looks like Ms. Tartaglia is up late."

**Panel 4:** "Awww yeahhh, take it off, baby."

**Panel 5:** "This telescope is really beginning to pay off."

**Panel 6:** (No text)

**Panel 7:** "Hey, come back."

**Panel 8:** "Aw, man! Oh well."

**Panel 9:** "Oh, look, Jupiter has moons."

**Notebook Page** (handwritten, partially illegible):
"let [Jupiter] and [other] paths validate motions / in [his] narrow aspect month [?] / [stick figures showing movement] / good heavens, [Jupiter] walk [illegible] beauties! / From population, pours just gets prime! / [mathematical symbols] / some promes about at [?] mm: ○ [he] 20, aha / have [?] constellations [illegible] and minute turn / [illegible] 40, ther he are ala [Jupiter] / [illegible] at modern calculate"

## Visual note
The telescope initially focuses on a woman at a window, suggesting voyeurism, but the final panel reveals the observer has actually been looking at Jupiter and its moons—shifting the punchline to an astronomical discovery. The "Galileo's lost notebook" parodies scientific documentation with crude sketches and garbled notes.

# Scene

A six-panel webcomic in black and white stick figure style. The top three panels show a technical presentation with diagrams, while the bottom three panels depict stick figures presenting to an audience of three seated figures.

# Text

Panel 1: "This machine would draw people to itself from all over the world."

Panel 2: "Then the machine would subdue the parts of their brains responsible for higher reasoning and executive function."

Panel 3: "And, simultaneously, it would stimulate the areas of the brain responsible for defensive rage and affective aggression."

Panel 4: "In theory, this should convert normally well-adjusted and reasonable individuals into raging douchebags."

Panel 5: "AND THEN WE COULD OBSERVE THE SUBJECTS LIKE RATS IN A CAGE, LOL."

Panel 6: "HOW LONG, MIRASTA?"

Panel 7: "WITH YOUR APPROVAL TODAY, WE COULD DEPLOY IN TEN WEEKS."

Panel 8: [Audience sits silently]

Panel 9: "DO IT."

Bottom caption: "HOW IT REALLY HAPPENED / the birth of the internet"

# Visual Note

The punchline depends on the image: a formal corporate pitch meeting presenting an elaborate machine designed to turn reasonable people into hostile, irrational individuals is revealed to be the origin story of the internet itself.

# Scene

A multi-panel webcomic in simple black-and-white line art. The first three panels show a stick figure teaching another about thermodynamics and internal combustion engines using equations and diagrams on a whiteboard. The final panel depicts the student as a truck driver, having forgotten practical advice about operating the vehicle.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
ΔS = Sy - Si = ∫ dQ/T
dQ = pdV + nCv dT
dQ/dt = nR dT/Cv + nCv dQ/dt

∫ dQ = ∫ f(n)dQ + ∫ f(n)Cv dQ/dt
ΔS = Sy - Si = nR [DIESEL CYCLE and OTTO CYCLE]

**Panel 2:**
"THERE, NOW YOU UNDERSTAND THE THERMODYNAMICS OF INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES."
ΔS = Sy - Si = nR

**Panel 3:**
"NOW YOU'RE READY, SON."
ΔS = Sy - Si = nR

**Panel 4:**
"REMEMBER, GO EASY ON THE CLUTCH OUT THERE."
"WHAT'S A CLUTCH?"

**Bottom caption:**
"entering the real world after college
it's kinda like this"

# Visual note

The humor depends on the contrast between the extensive theoretical instruction shown in the first three panels and the student's complete ignorance of a basic practical detail about vehicle operation in the final panel.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in black ink on white background. The first six panels depict two characters on a cliff having a conversation. The final two panels show an aerial view of a desert landscape with cacti, mountains, and a sign reading "Friend Zone," with one character having fallen from above.

# Text
**Panel 1:**
Character 1: "WHERE ARE YOU TAKING ME?"
Character 2: "ALMOST THERE."

**Panel 2:**
Character 2: "THERE. SEE? ISN'T IT A BEAUTIFUL VIEW?"
Character 1: "WOW! INCREDIBLE."

**Panel 3:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 4:**
Character 1: "Y'KNOW, I REALLY LIKE YOU."
Character 2: "I REALLY LIKE YOU TOO."

**Panel 5:**
Character 1: "I THINK YOU'RE A REALLY NICE GUY."
Character 2: "THANKS. I THINK YOU'RE A REALLY NICE... WAIT A MINUTE..."

**Panel 6:**
Character 1: "AND SOME DAY YOU'RE GONNA MAKE SOME LUCKY GIRL REALLY HAPPY."
Character 2: "NO! STOP! STOP TALKING."

**Panels 7-8:**
"Many fall. Few escape."

# Visual Note
Character 2 is pushed off the cliff during the "friend zone" speech and falls through the subsequent panels, landing in the desert below near the "Friend Zone" sign. The final caption is a dark joke about the commonality of rejection.

**Scene:** A minimalist diagram on a black background displays two logarithmic frequency scales with labeled sensory ranges, illustrating the narrow band of human perception.

**Text:**
- Y-axis label: "ELECTROMAGNETIC SPECTRUM FREQUENCY (Hz)"
- X-axis label: "SOUND FREQUENCY (Hz)"
- Y-axis scale: 10^8, 10^6, 10^12
- X-axis scale: 10^-6, 10^-3, 1, 10^3, 10^6
- "RANGE OF HUMAN HEARING" (bracketed around 10^3 Hz)
- "RANGE OF HUMAN VISION" (bracketed around 10^14 Hz)
- Arrow annotation: "ALL YOU HEAR AND ALL YOU SEE ALL HAPPENS IN HERE"
- Caption: "In the grand scheme of things, we're all pretty much blind and deaf."
- Credit: "ZACH WEINER"

**Visual note:** The diagram uses a logarithmic scale to show that human sensory ranges occupy an infinitesimally small portion of the full frequency spectrum, creating a visual metaphor for human perceptual limitations.

# Scene

A six-panel stick figure webcomic in black and white depicting a narrator reflecting on their friend Chucky's uninhibited dancing at clubs. The narrator stands apart, often wearing a blue cap, while observing Chucky's chaotic movements on the dance floor.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"So I used to go clubbing a lot with my friend Chucky."
[CHUCKY]
"He has got to be the worst dancer I've ever seen... ever!"

**Panel 2:**
"But he always runs to the center of the dance floor and just dances all by himself."

**Panel 3:**
"Just look at that fool. He has absolutely no rhythm or coordination."
[THE RUNNING MAN]
"WTF?"

**Panel 4:**
"He looks like that alien from that movie..."
"...with its tentacles flailing all about wildly."

**Panel 5:**
"And check out *this* move! Flapping his arms like an idiot."
"What the hell is he thinking?"

**Panel 6:**
"I would never embarrass myself like Chucky does."
"Either he doesn't know how stupid he looks, or he just doesn't care."
"Either way, it saddens me to know that I will never be that free."

# Visual Note

The final panel contrasts the narrator standing isolated with awareness of social judgment against Chucky dancing freely and unselfconsciously among others, illustrating the narrator's self-imposed constraint.

# Stealth Time-Traveling Historians From the Future

## Scene
A stick-figure webcomic in a 3x4 grid. The setting progresses from a classroom with a teacher and three students, to a futuristic time portal, to a historical figure at a desk in the past. Simple black lines on white background with minimal detail.

## Text

**Panel 1:**
"the future..."
"OK, CLASS. FIELD TRIP TODAY."
"YAY!"

**Panel 2:**
"THANKS TO THE INVENTION OF STEALTH TIME TRAVEL, WE CAN VISIT ANY POINT IN THE PAST."

**Panel 3:**
"AND WE CAN OBSERVE THE LIVES OF HISTORICAL FIGURES WITHOUT BEING DETECTED."

**Panel 4:**
"SO EVERYONE DIRECT YOUR ATTENTION TO THE TIME PORTAL."

**Panel 5:**
"TODAY WE WILL OBSERVE THE MOST FAMOUS SCIENTIST OF THE 21ST CENTURY."

**Panel 6:**
"WE WILL ACTUALLY WITNESS THE EXACT MOMENTS BEFORE HE UNIFIED GENERAL RELATIVITY AND QUANTUM PHYSICS."

**Panel 7:**
"TIME TO WITNESS HISTORY."

**Panel 8:**
[Silent panel]

**Panel 9:**
"HMMM. HE'S A BIT OF A SLOB, ISN'T HE?"

**Panel 10:**
"OH, DEAR."
"BURP"

**Panel 11:**
[Silent panel]

**Panel 12:**
"CLASS DISMISSED! CLASS DISMISSED!"
"FAP FAP FAP"

**Caption:**
"STEALTH TIME-TRAVELING HISTORIANS FROM THE FUTURE
the reason I'm afraid of becoming famous"

## Visual note
The humor contrasts the students' expectation of witnessing a profound scientific breakthrough with the reality of observing the scientist burping and being slovenly. The teacher's panicked dismissal and running sound effects suggest the scientist has detected them and is chasing them away.

comics.title: Fairy Tale

# Scene

A black-and-white line drawing of a laboratory interior with curved floor and three back windows. The room contains scientific equipment—flasks, beakers, measuring cylinders, microscope, Bunsen burner, and various apparatus—interspersed with fairy-tale figures including a winged fairy and characters in classical dress. The sketch-like, cartoonish style emphasizes an imaginative blend of science and wonder.

# Text

"A scientist in his laboratory is not only a technician: he is also a child placed before natural phenomena which impress him like a fairy tale.

— Marie Curie"

# Visual Note

The composition literally illustrates the Curie quote by depicting actual fairy-tale characters integrated among laboratory equipment, visually representing the coexistence of scientific inquiry and imagination.

# Scene

A minimalist stick-figure comic with simple black lines and blue coloring. A character with a round blue head sits at a table with groceries and coffee. The comic escalates from a domestic observation to an absurdist cosmic visualization, then returns to the character sitting silently at the table.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"HMM. WEIRD. I RAN OUT OF CEREAL, MILK, BREAD, AND COFFEE ALL AT SAME TIME."

**Panel 2:**
"I WONDER WHAT THE PROBABILITY IS OF THIS HAPPENING ON ANY RANDOMLY CHOSEN DAY OF THE YEAR."

**Panel 3:**
"BASED ON MY SHOPPING AND CONSUMPTION PATTERNS... LET'S SEE... CARRY THE 3..."

**Panel 4:**
"0.000312!
HOLY COW!"

**Panel 5:**
"IT'S LIKE THE LAWS OF PROBABILITY ARE GOING HIGGLEDY-PIGGLEDY!"

**Panel 6:**
"IT COULD MEAN THAT THE VERY FABRIC OF THE UNIVERSE IS COMING APART AT THE SEAMS IN THIS VERY ROOM!!!"

**Panel 7:**
"IT COULD MEAN THAT THIS POINT IN TIME INHERENTLY CONTAINS SOME SORT OF COSMIC SIGNIFICANCE!

ALMOST AS IF IT WERE THE TEMPORAL JUNCTION POINT OF THE ENTIRE SPACETIME CONTINUUM!!!

GREAT SCOTT!"

**Panel 8:**
(Character sits at table)

**Panel 9:**
"ON THE OTHER HAND, IT COULD JUST BE AN AMAZING COINCIDENCE."

# Visual Note

The comic escalates from mundane observation to cosmic catastrophe with increasingly dramatic language, only to deflate completely in the final panel with a simple, anticlimactic alternative explanation.

# Scene

A nine-panel stick figure webcomic in black and white line art. Two adults wearing sunglasses and a child in a beanie hat navigate various domestic situations, with the adults progressively hiding items and closing off areas of their home as the child moves through the house.

# Text

"If you have nothing to hide, you have nothing to fear. Amirite?"

# Visual Note

The comic's humor relies on the sequence showing the adults increasingly concealing items, closing doors, and creating barriers as the child moves through the house, before putting the child to bed. This visually demonstrates the irony of the final statement: innocent people naturally hide things from children, which contradicts the logic of the quoted phrase.

# Scene
A figure in academic robes stands frustrated before a blackboard completely covered with complex electromagnetic and magnetic field equations. The art style is simple line-drawing.

# Text
**Equations on blackboard:**

I ds × (4πr)c, R²
∫c, (I'ds × R̂)/R² = (μ₀/4π) ∫c, (I'ds × R)/R³
Fc'→c = ∮c I ds × B(r)

Um = (1/2μ₀) ∫(∇×B) · A dτ
m = (1/2μ₀) ∫c B² dτ - (1/2μ₀) ∮c (A×B) · da
Um = ∫μu (B²/2μ₀) dτ
um = B²/(2μ₀)
Um = ∫ALL um dτ

dF = I ds × B(r)
dB(r) = (μ₀/4π) (I'ds × R̂)/R²
dA = (μ₀/4π) (dm × R̂)/R² = (μ₀/4π)
A(r) = (μ₀/4π) ∫N ((∇' × M))/R
= (μ₀/4π) ∫V ((∇' × M) dτ)/R
A(r) = (μ₀/4π) ∫V (Jm(r'd))/R

∇·D = ρf
∇×E = -∂B/∂t
∇·B = 0
∇×H = Jf + ∂D/∂t

**Caption:** "I have no idea what I'm doing."

# Visual Note
The humor comes from the stark contrast between the extensive, complex equations filling the entire blackboard and the character's admission of complete confusion.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene
A stick-figure comic in black ink on white background. A simple circular-headed character with stick limbs progresses through panels wearing a mask and sunglasses, culminating in a final panel showing the character in bed rendered in blue with a thought bubble above.

## Text

Panel 1: "HMMM. WHAT SHOULD I DO TODAY?"

Panel 2: "I KNOW! I THINK I'LL MURDER SOME RANDOM GUY."

Panel 3: "FIRST I NEED A MURDERING MASK."

Panel 4: "NOW I NEED TO PUT ON MY MURDERING SHOES."

Panel 5: "AND, FINALLY, I NEED A MURDERING KNIFE."

Panel 6: "THEN PICK SOME RANDOM HOUSE IN A LOW-CRIME NEIGHBORHOOD."

Panel 8: "NOW I'M GONNA WAIT BEHIND THIS SHOWER CURTAIN FOR HOURS."

## Visual Note
The final panel reveals the preceding narrative is a bedtime thought, reframing the elaborate murder plan as an anxious nighttime fantasy.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A Gmail composition window is open with an email addressed to Jenny with the subject "<3 <3 <3". A Google warning dialog box has appeared over the compose area, interrupting the send action.

# Text

**Toolbar:**
SEND | Save Now | Discard

**Email header:**
To: jenny [REDACTED]@g...
Add Cc | Add Bcc
Subject: <3 <3 <3
Attach a file

**Email body:**
"Dearest Jenny,

I can't stop thinking about you. I had that dream about you again last night. Y'know that dream where I am Captain Picard and you are the Borg? I miss you so much. Why didn't you return my calls last night? I tried calling you several times last night but I kept getting your voicemail. I know you were home last night because of your facebook and twitter posts. Are you upset with me or something? I hope I didn't do anything to upset you. Please write back soon. I think you are the one. I think we should get married. What do you think?"

**Warning dialog:**
"The page at https://mail.google.com says:

You have only been dating Jenny for a week.

You wrote a message that makes you sound needy and a little bit crazy. Send anyway?"

[OK] [Cancel]

# Visual Note

Gmail's automated warning system detects the obsessive and desperate tone of the email—mentioning repeated unanswered calls, surveillance of social media, and a marriage proposal after one week of dating—and interrupts the send with a reality check.

# Scene

A stick-figure character is hit by an object labeled "3" and transported into a mathematical singularity where impossible concepts become possible. Various characters explain abstract mathematical and philosophical ideas before revealing they are game developers avoiding discussion of Half-Life 3.

# Text

**Panel 4:**
Sound effect: "BLAT!"

**Panel 5:**
Character: "HEY, WHERE AM I?"

**Panel 6:**
Character in suit: "WELCOME TO THE CHRONOSYNCASTIC INFUNDIBULUM, A PLACE WHERE INFINITY ENDS."
Other character: "WHAT?"

**Panel 7:**
Character in suit: "YOU ARE NOW INSIDE A MATHEMATICAL SINGULARITY WHERE INFINITY ENDS."
Other character: "WOW REALLY?"

**Panel 8:**
Character: "LOOK, SEE? TWO PARALLEL LINES MEETING IN EUCLIDEAN SPACE."
Other character: "COOL!"

**Panel 9:**
Character in suit: "AND THESE ARE THE LAST SEVERAL DIGITS OF PI."
Text displayed: "33462648323397985353629514135"
Other character: "WOW! IT'S A PALINDROME!"

**Panel 10:**
Character in suit: "AND, NOW, FLYING CARS ARE THE NORM."

**Panel 11:**
Character in suit: "HERE, THE KOCH SNOWFLAKE IS EVERYWHERE DIFFERENTIABLE. THERE IS NO HALTING PROBLEM, AND AMERICANS HAVE ADOPTED THE METRIC SYSTEM."
Other character: "YOU'RE KIDDING!"

**Panel 12:**
Character in suit: "THIS PLACE IS AMAZING."

**Panel 13:**
Character: "BUT WHO ARE THOSE GUYS?"
Character in suit: "THEM? OH... UH... GAME DEVELOPERS."

**Panel 14:**
Character in suit: "WE DON'T LIKE TO TALK ABOUT IT."
Other character: "PERFECT. HALF-LIFE 3 MUST BE PERFECT!"

# Visual note

The digits of pi displayed in Panel 9 form a palindrome, emphasizing the impossible nature of this mathematical space where contradictions coexist.

# Webcomic Transcription

**Scene:** A minimalist black and white comic with rust-red Martian soil. An astronaut stands alone on Mars in the early panels, experiencing wonder. Later panels show mission control operators at their stations, frustrated. The tone shifts from poetic reverence to absurdist humor.

**Text:**

Panel 1: "So beautiful."

Panel 2: "This very day, Great Mars, I traverse thy desolate plains,"

Panel 3: "And wander lonely as a cloud, bereft of darkened rains."

Panel 4: "How many weary steps upon rusted soil o'ergone. How far the travels from Man's first dawn."

Panel 5: "If sight could sing, o what composeth the eyes, Both rejoice the splendor whence songs arise."

Panel 6: "Great Mars, what majestic secrets unrevealed still, What mysteries doth thou shroud under hill?"

Panel 7: "O, spark of life that struggles to be free,"

Panel 8: "On Earthen fertile crescent, why should this a desert be?"

Panel 9: "To see the Universe in a grain of sand,"

Panel 10: [MISSION CONTROL label] "WHAT THE HELL IS HE DOING NOW?" "SHOULDN'T HE BE DOING A SOIL ANALYSIS?"

Bottom caption: "Never send a poet to do a scientist's job."

**Visual note:** The astronaut's increasingly elaborate poetic soliloquy contrasts sharply with mission control's frustrated realization that soil analysis is being neglected.

"?"

# Scene

A nine-panel webcomic in simple stick-figure style featuring a character with a blue cap. The comic uses minimalist black line drawings against white backgrounds to depict a story about superstition and regret.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"One night, I was having trouble falling asleep."

**Panel 2:**
"So I got up and decided to play a couple of hands of solitaire poker."

**Panel 3:**
"Almost immediately, I dealt myself a royal flush."
"WOO - HOO !"

**Panel 4:**
"ODDS AGAINST 649,739:1"
"TODAY MUST BE MY LUCKY DAY !"

**Panel 5:**
"But then it occurred to me."

**Panel 6:**
"What if I just wasted my entire lifetime's supply of good luck on one useless poker hand? ...alone in my apartment."

**Panel 7:**
"SON OF A..."

**Panel 8:**
[Silent panel]

**Panel 9:**
"My life has sucked ever since."
"NOOOOOOOOO"
"True story."

# Visual Note

The character's emotional progression from celebration to despair is shown through increasingly distressed expressions and exclamations, culminating in the revelation that a single stroke of luck triggered a lifetime of misfortune.

# Scene
Two stick figures lie on their backs on green ground at night, surrounded by stars, gazing upward and discussing the cosmos.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
JUST THINK HOW MANY CIVILIZATIONS MUST EXIST OUT THERE IN THE UNIVERSE. THERE MUST BE MILLIONS.

**Panel 2:**
LOOK, THERE COULD BE A CIVILIZATION ON A PLANET ORBITING THAT STAR RIGHT THERE.

**Panel 3:**
IN FACT, THERE'S PROBABLY AN ALIEN ON THAT PLANET LOOKING UP AT THE STARS RIGHT NOW.

**Panel 4:**
AND HE'S PROBABLY WONDERING IF...
ACTUALLY, THAT'S SUPERNOVA 1987A.
WHAT?

**Panel 5:**
IT'S THE REMNANT OF A HUGE STELLAR EXPLOSION IN THE LARGE MAGELLANIC CLOUD.

**Panel 6:**
ANY CIVILIZATION IN THAT STAR SYSTEM WOULD MOST LIKELY HAVE BEEN VIOLENTLY OBLITERATED 168,000 YEARS AGO.

**Panel 7:**
(No text)

**Panel 8:**
COOL!

**Panel 9:**
A little knowledge makes everything a little cooler.

# Visual Note
The humor comes from the deflation of romantic wonder—the speaker expects disappointment upon learning the star is a deadly supernova remnant, but their companion instead finds this fact *more* fascinating than the original thought.

**Scene:** A minimalist line-drawn character with a blue head sits in bed reading. The sequence shows the character encountering an unfamiliar word, considering options, and ultimately deciding against consulting a dictionary on the floor.

# Scene

A stick-figure comic set in heaven. A large figure labeled God with a halo stands before three smaller figures (angels) in blue. The art style uses simple black line drawings on a white background.

# Text

**Panel 1 (Header):** "...in heaven"

**Panel 1:**
Caption: "HERE ARE THE FINAL HARDWARE SPECS FOR THE UNIVERSE."

**Panel 2:**
God: "GET STARTED PROGRAMMING THE LAWS OF PHYSICS IMMEDIATELY."
Angel: "YES, LORD. WE'LL GET ST... WAIT."

**Panel 3:**
Angel: "THIS CAN'T BE RIGHT. THERE'S ONLY 64OK OF MEMORY?"

**Panel 4:**
God: "YEAH, THAT'S RIGHT. THAT OUGHT TO BE ENOUGH FOR ANYTHING."

**Panel 5:**
God: "BUT THIS WILL SERIOUSLY LIMIT THE ACCURACY WITH WHICH THE PROGRAM CAN SIMULTANEOUSLY CALCULATE THE POSITION AND MOMENTUM OF ANY PARTICLE."

**Panel 6:**
Angel: "YEAH, SO? YOU GOT A PROBLEM WITH THAT?"
God: "UH... NO."

**Panel 7:**
God: "GREAT. GET TO WORK CHOP CHOP!"
Angel: "YES, LORD."

**Panel 8:**
Angel: "THIS IS GONNA CONFUSE THE HUMANS."

# Visual Note

The comic explains Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle as a result of the universe being programmed with insufficient memory, treating physical laws as hardware constraints rather than fundamental truths.

# Scene

A stick-figure webcomic showing a man's progression from conspiracy theorist to infiltrator to disillusioned operative. The art style uses simple black-and-white stick figures with minimalist settings including offices, pyramids, and corporate buildings.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"As I grew older, it became increasingly clear to me that the world was run by evil cabal of elites."
"THEY'RE COLLAPSING OUR ECONOMY AND DESTROYING OUR FUTURE. NOBODY COULD BE THAT INCOMPETENT. IT MUST BE AN EVIL PLAN."
Label: "WALL ST TIMES"

**Panel 2:**
"They lie to us and manipulate us through the mainstream media; they continually dumb us down with TV shows devoid of intellectual content."
"ONLY AN EVIL CONSPIRACY COULD PRODUCE SUCH VAPID CRAPPY TV."

**Panel 3:**
"I concluded that we were all just slaves of the Illuminati."
"ALEX JONES WAS RIGHT."

**Panel 4:**
"I decided that I needed to fight back."
"BUT, I SHALL... BE..."

**Panel 5:**
"FREE!"

**Panel 6:**
"But the Illuminati can only be defeated from the inside. I needed to infiltrate their organization."

**Panel 7:**
"I got my foot in the door as a lowly mail clerk in one of their evil illuminational corporations."

**Panel 8:**
"I worked my way up the ranks with promotion after promotion..."

**Panel 9:**
"I worked hard and made the right connections."

**Panel 10:**
"I studied their evil ways and learned their techniques."

**Panel 11:**
"Until... FINALLY... I made it to the top."
Label: "MA BOSSYPANTS"

**Panel 12:**
"I WAS the Illuminati."

**Panel 13:**
"I decided not to waste any time."
"SMITHERS!"
"YES, SIR?"

**Panel 14:**
"I put my plan into effect immediately."
"IT'S TIME TO END THIS."

**Panel 15:**
"IT'S TIME TO END THE CONSPIRACY OF DESTROYING THE ECONOMY AND DUMBING DOWN THE POPULATION."
"THE LINE MUST BE DRAWN HERE!"

**Panel 16:**
"THIS FAR, NO FURTHER!"

**Panel 17:**
[No dialogue]

**Panel 18:**
"BUT, SIR, THERE IS NO CONSPIRACY."

**Panel 19:**
"OH... OKAY."

**Panel 20:**
"THAT WILL BE ALL, SMITHERS."
"YES, SIR."

**Panel 21–22:**
[No dialogue]

**Panel 23:**
"I decided to weep for humanity."

# Visual Note

The silent panels (21–22) create an awkward pause that emphasizes the anticlimactic collapse of the character's grand plans upon learning the conspiracy does not exist.

comics.title: Many World Problem
comics.comment: Somewhere there is a parallel universe where contradictions don't exist.
comics.commentHtml: Somewhere there is a parallel universe where contradictions don't exist.
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A nine-panel black and white stick figure comic depicting two characters discussing parallel universes and quantum mechanics. The art style is minimalist line drawings with simple circular heads and stick bodies, set in an abstract non-specific environment.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"IT WORKED! MY INVENTION WORKS!"

**Panel 2:**
"I HAVE TRAVELED TO A PARALLEL UNIVERSE."

**Panel 3:**
"HEY, IT'S ME!"
"HUH?"

**Panel 4:**
"I'M YOU FROM A PARALLEL UNIVERSE. I'VE INVENTED A PARALLEL UNIVERSE MACHINE."
"WOW, REALLY?"

**Panel 5:**
"THAT'S AMAZING. BUT IT'S TOO BAD YOU'RE STUCK HERE NOW."
"WHAT?"

**Panel 6:**
"Y'SEE, IN THIS UNIVERSE, THE MANY-WORLDS INTERPRETATION OF QUANTUM MECHANICS IS FALSE."

**Panel 7:**
"IN THIS UNIVERSE, YOUR UNIVERSE DOESN'T EXIST."

**Panel 8:**
"OH NO... THAT MEANS THAT... WAIT. WAIT A MINUTE."

**Panel 9:**
"THAT DOESN'T EVEN MAKE SENSE. NEITHER DOES RUSSELL'S PARADOX, YET IT EXISTS."

# Visual Note

The joke relies on a logical contradiction: if the alternate universe doesn't exist in this universe's physics, the traveling character shouldn't exist either, creating a self-referential paradox that the character acknowledges by comparing it to Russell's Paradox.

# Scene

A nine-panel stick figure comic depicting a conversation between three characters. The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white background. Each panel shows the same three figures in conversation, with speech bubbles indicating who is speaking.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"THE WIFE AND KIDS ARE DRIVING ME CRAZY."

**Panel 2:**
"I CAN'T FIND A MOMENT'S PEACE TO MYSELF. IS THAT TOO MUCH TO ASK?"
"I HEAR YA, BRAH."

**Panel 3:**
"AND LITTLE BILLY HAS BEEN ACTING UP IN SCHOOL AGAIN. THAT KID IS NOTHING BUT TROUBLE."

**Panel 4:**
"I WISH I HAD A WIFE AND FAMILY."

**Panel 5:**
"AND MY BOSS IS KILLING ME AT WORK."

**Panel 6:**
"HE'S ALWAYS ALL UP MY ASS ABOUT THOSE DAMN TPS REPORTS."

**Panel 7:**
"I WISH I HAD A STEADY JOB."
"AND WHAT'S WITH THIS HOUSING MARKET?"

**Panel 8:**
"I HAVE A $400,000 MORTGAGE ON A HOUSE THAT JUST APPRAISED AT $226,000. THAT'S MESSED UP, BRAH."
"I WISH I COULD AFFORD TO OWN A HOUSE."

**Panel 9:**
"I DON'T KNOW HOW MUCH MORE OF THIS I CAN TAKE."
"WHEN YOU'RE YOUNG, NOBODY TELLS YOU HOW MUCH LIFE IS GONNA SUCK."
"I WISH I HAD A LIFE WORTH COMPLAINING ABOUT."

# Visual Note

Each character complains about their circumstances while the character to their left expresses envy for those exact same circumstances, creating a cyclical pattern of dissatisfaction across all nine panels.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A multi-panel webcomic in simple stick-figure style depicting a conversation between a father figure wearing glasses and his son about academic work and the Riemann Hypothesis. The comic progresses from a home office setting through various confrontational scenes, parodies a military aviation film, and concludes with commentary on why mathematicians are rarely subjects of movies.

# Text
**Panel 1:**
Father: "WORD ON THE STREET IS YOU'RE WORKING ON THE RIEMANN HYPOTHESIS."

**Panel 2:**
Father: "WHAT YOU SHOULD BE DOING IS WORKING ON YOUR THESIS!"

**Panel 3:**
Father: "SON, YOUR EGO IS WRITING CHECKS YOUR BRAIN CAN'T CASH."

**Panel 4:**
Son: "THE NAMES OF EVERY FIELD'S MEDAL WINNER ARE UP ON THAT PLAQUE."

**Panel 5:**
Father: "DO YOU THINK YOUR NAME WILL BE ON THAT PLAQUE?"
Son: "YES, SIR."

**Panel 6:**
Father: "THAT'S PRETTY ARROGANT CONSIDERING THE COMPANY YOU'RE IN."
Son: "I LIKE THAT IN A MATHEMATICIAN."

**Panel 7:**
Son working intently at a blackboard with mathematical equation.

**Panel 8:**
Father: "YOU REALLY ARE A COWBOY."

**Panel 9:**
Father: "WHAT'S YOUR PROBLEM, KAZANSKI?"

**Panel 10:**
Father: "YOU'RE EVERYONE'S PROBLEM. DON'T LIKE YOU BECAUSE YOUR PROOFS LACK RIGOR."

**Panel 11:**
Son: "THAT'S RIGHT! ICE. MAN. I AM DANGEROUS."

**Panel 12:**
Father: "THAT'S ACTUALLY NOT WHAT I SAID."

**Panel 13:**
Crowd: "CONGRATULATIONS ON SOLVING RIEMANN, YOU CAN FLY WITH THE 'ET AL' ANY TIME."

**Panel 14:**
Son: "BULLSHIT! YOU CAN BE MINE."

**Panel 15:**
Multiple voices: "YAY YAY YAY"
Text: "ETERNAL SALVATION"

**Final panel:**
"THE END"
"There's a reason why they don't make a lot of movies about mathematicians."

# Visual note
The comic parodies a military aviation film, with the father as commanding officer and son as arrogant pilot. The dialogue references dangerous flying and replaces "Top Gun" with "et al." The son misinterprets criticism about his proofs lacking "rigor" as a personal attack, triggering an ego response that mirrors fighter-pilot dramatics.

# Scene

A two-panel webcomic in simple black and white line art showing the OSI model layers stacked vertically on both the left and right sides, with a woman on the left and a man on the right. Below each stack, illustrations progress from abstract representations of communication downward, culminating in the two figures exchanging a heart symbol at the bottom.

# Text

**Left stack (top to bottom):**
LAYER 1 PHYSICAL
LAYER 2 DATA LINK
LAYER 3 NETWORK
LAYER 4 TRANSPORT
LAYER 5 SESSION
LAYER 6 PRESENTATION
LAYER 7 APPLICATION

**Right stack (top to bottom):**
LAYER 1 PHYSICAL
LAYER 2 DATA LINK
LAYER 3 NETWORK
LAYER 4 TRANSPORT
LAYER 5 SESSION
LAYER 6 PRESENTATION
LAYER 7 APPLICATION

**Bottom panel labels:**
LAYER 3
LAYER 7
LAYER 4
LAYER 1

# Visual Note

The comic parallels romantic communication with the OSI model's layered structure, showing how complex abstract interactions at higher layers (presentation and application) are encoded down through physical transmission at lower layers, then decoded back up at the receiving end. Despite the technical complexity of all intermediate layers, the final message simplifies to a heart.

comics.title: Arithmetic for Beginners
comics.comment: I have also intentionally included fatal mistakes in the equations to make it more realistic. Please see the errata page on our website.
comics.commentHtml: I have also intentionally included fatal mistakes in the equations to make it more realistic.  Please see the errata page on our website.
comics.transcript:
# Scene
A webcomic contrasting a simple, child-friendly explanation of basic arithmetic using line-drawn rabbits with an abruptly complex mathematical derivation featuring advanced notation and multi-line equations.

# Text

Suppose you have one rabbit.

Now suppose someone gives you one more rabbit.

Now, if you count your rabbits, you have two rabbits. So one rabbit plus one rabbit equals two rabbits. So one plus one equals two.

1 + 1 = 2

And that is how arithmetic is done.

Now that you understand the basic idea behind arithmetic, let's take a look at a simple easy-to-understand example that puts into practice what we just learned.

**Try It Out Example 1.7**

log Π(N) = (N + 1/2) log N − N + A − ∫[N to ∞] B₁(x)dx/x,     A = 1 + ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/x

log Π(s) = (s + 1/2) log s − s + A − ∫[0 to ∞] B₁(t)dt/(t + s)

log Π(s) = lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + Σ[n=1 to N] log n − Σ[n=1 to N] log(s + n)]

= lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + ∫[1 to N] log x dx − 1/2 log N + ∫[N to 1] B₁(x)dx/x − ∫[1 to N] log(s + x)dx − 1/2[log(s + 1) + log(s + N)] − ∫[1 to N] B₁(x)dx/(s + x)]

= lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + N log N − N + 1 + 1/2 log N + ∫[1 to N] B₁(x)dx/x − (s + N)log(s + N) + (s + N) + (s + 1)log(s + 1) − (s + 1) − 1/2 log(s + 1) − 1/2 log(s + N) − ∫[1 to N] B₁(x)dx/(s + x)]

= (s + 1/2) log(s + 1) + ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/x − ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/(s + x) + lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + (N + 1/2) log N − (s + N + 1/2) log(s + N)]

= (s + 1/2) log(s + 1) + (A − 1) − ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/(s + x) + lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1)/(1 + s/N) + (N + 1/2) log(1 + s/N)]

If the authors of computer programming books wrote arithmetic textbooks...

# Visual note
The humor depends on the extreme visual and conceptual disconnect between the promised "simple easy-to-understand example" and the highly advanced mathematical derivation that immediately follows.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist illustrated adaptation of Walt Whitman's "Sea-Drift," featuring a figure in green positioned throughout maritime and celestial settings. The panels alternate between colored seascapes and black backgrounds with white text, emphasizing solitude against vast horizons.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
In cabin'd ships at sea,

**Panel 2:**
The boundless blue on every side expanding,

**Panel 3:**
With whistling winds and music of the waves, the large imperious waves,

**Panel 4:**
Or some lone bark buoy'd on the dense marine,
Where joyous full of faith, spreading white sails,
She cleaves the ether mid the sparkle and the foam of day, or under many a star at night,

**Panel 5:**
By sailors young and old haply will I, a reminiscence of the land, be read,

**Panel 6:**
In full rapport at last.

**Text section:**
Here are our thoughts, voyagers' thoughts,
Here not the land, from land, alone appears, may then by them be said,
The sky o'eraches here, we feel the undulating deck beneath our feet,
We feel the long pulsation, ebb and flow of endless motion,
The tones of unseen mystery, the vague and vast suggestions of the briny world, the liquid-flowing syllables,
The perfume, the faint creaking of the cordage, the melancholy rhythm,
The boundless vista and the horizon far and dim are all here,
And this is ocean's poem.

**Panel:**
Then falter not O book, fulfil your destiny,
You not a reminiscence of the land alone,
You too as a lone bark cleaving the ether, purpos'd I know not whether, yet ever full of faith,

**Panel:**
Consort to every ship that sails, sail you!
Bear forth to them fold'd my love, (dear mariners, for you I fold it here in every leaf)
Speed on my book! spread your white sails my little bark athwart the imperious waves,

**Final panels:**
Chant on, sail on,
bear o'er the boundless blue from me to every sea,

This song for mariners and all their ships.
—Walt Whitman
1871

# Visual Note

The book is visually depicted as a ship sailing through space and ocean, literalizing the metaphor of the poem itself as a vessel. The figure in green appears consistently at vastly reduced scale against expansive seascapes and celestial bodies, emphasizing human smallness within nature's immensity. A ship's wheel appears in one panel, mirroring the cyclical nature of voyages and reading.

# Cyanide & Happiness Comic Transcription

**Scene:** A stick-figure comic in black, blue, and red ink depicting an office worker's elaborate escape plan to leave work early. The comic includes introductory panels, two floor plan diagrams, and a sequential "Operation Jailbreak" sequence.

**Text:**

"I've finished my work and it's not 5 o'clock yet."

"If I want to leave early without running into my boss, I have to plan carefully."

"I know that my boss is giving a presentation in room 103 until 4:45."

"Afterwards, she will most likely visit Dr. Erickson's lab for a couple of minutes."

"Then she'll come back up to the 4th floor via stairwell 1."

"So I'll have a brief window during which I'll have to sprint down the hallway."

"And make my way to stairwell 2."

"Operation Jailbreak is in effect."

"HEY, WHERE ARE YOU GOING?"

"OH, uh...I WAS JUST...uh...Y'know..."

"OKAY, HAVE A GREAT WEEKEND."

"My boss is an evil wizard."

**Visual note:** The elaborate planning sequence is undercut by the boss simply heading in the opposite direction, and the numbered countdown panels (4, 3, 2) create an ironic structure that emphasizes the anticlimactic resolution.

# Scene
A stick-figure programmer sits at a computer, progressing through nine panels of dialogue with a colleague. Simple black lines on white background depict the interaction.

# Text

Panel 1:
"SMART POINTERS ARE FOR WIMPS. REAL PROGRAMMERS GO RAW."

Panel 2:
"REAL PROGRAMMERS ALWAYS KEEP TOTAL CONTROL OF MEMORY."

Panel 3:
"IN FACT, REAL PROGRAMMERS DON'T EVEN HAVE TO CHECK FOR BAD MEMORY ALLOCATIONS."

Panel 4:
"THAT'S RIGHT. I AM THE BAD BOY OF PROGRAMMING. I'M GONNA LIVE FAST, DIE EARLY, AND CODE CLOSE TO THE METAL."

Panel 5:
"YOU!!"
[?]

Panel 6:
"THE CODING STANDARDS MANUAL. READ IT! LEARN IT! LOVE IT!"

Panel 7:
"WE'RE TIRED OF CORRECTING YOUR MISTAKES. YOU'RE SLOWING DOWN THE ENTIRE TEAM."

Panel 8:
"GET YOUR ACT TOGETHER."

Panel 9:
"WE BAD BOYS ARE OFTEN MISUNDERSTOOD."
[SIGH *SMASH*]

# Visual Note
The programmer's confident boasting about reckless memory management is immediately undercut by a colleague confronting him with a coding standards manual, revealing his "bad boy" attitude is simply creating work for others.

comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription: "An Adventure in the Wilderness"

**Scene:**
A minimalist stick-figure comic in black ink depicting a character's childhood adventure into the wilderness and reflective journey home. The narrative follows the character from their decision to explore, through various obstacles, to contemplation in bed at night.

**Text:**

Panel 1: "One day, when I was 4 years old, I decided that I wanted to go on an adventure in the wilderness."

Panel 2: "The first order of business was to procure a supply of food."

Panel 3: "One can never go wrong with cheese curls."

Panel 4: "With a sandwich bag full of life-sustaining cheese curls in hand, I set out into the unfamiliar."

Panel 5: "Immediately, I chanced upon what appeared to be an abandoned trail, possibly built by an ancient race long forgotten."

Panel 6: "Just imagine the adventures that occurred along this trail... lost in the sands of time."

Panel 7: "It wasn't long before the elements mercilessly tried to force my return home."

Panel 8: "Perhaps the gods have been angered by my brazen attempt to explore forbidden lands."

Panel 9: "I soldiered on. Many perils were encountered and overcome. Eventually, I reached the end of the known world."

Panel 10 (Map): "SCARY FOREST" / "END OF THE KNOWN WORLD" / "MY HOUSE" / "START"

Panel 11: "Beyond this place be mysteries begging to be explored."

Panel 12: "But first I needed rest."

Panel 13: "I found a dry patch of ground under an old board."

Panels 14-16: "And I feasted on the good food." [with "CHOMP" sound effects]

Panel 17: "There were still many uncharted lands awaiting discovery..."

Panel 18: "...but the sun was sinking fast."

Panel 19: "And the wilderness can be an unforgiving place at night."

Panel 20: "Reluctantly, I started my long trek back home."

Panel 21: [Silhouette transition panels]

Panel 22: "Safe at home again. Cold and tired."

Panel 23: "Sleep did not come easy that night."

Panel 24: "For I had briefly tasted the sweet nectar of independence."

Panel 25: "And it lingered."

**Visual note:**
The map reveals the character only explored their immediate neighborhood, contrasting sharply with the epic language used to describe it. Silhouette panels during the return home emphasize the growing darkness and danger of the wilderness at night.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A classroom in simple black and white stick-figure style. A female student sits among rows of other students during a literature discussion. The comic escalates from a straightforward classroom debate into an internal spiral of increasingly dark thoughts, ending with the student alone in bed at night.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"CAN ANYONE TELL ME WHAT THIS POEM MEANS?"

**Panel 2:**
(Silent)

**Panel 3:**
"YES, SEAN?"

**Panel 4:**
"I BELIEVE THAT THE FLOWERS IN THIS POEM REPRESENT THE BLOSSOMING OF INNOCENCE IN THE GROWING FACE OF THE DICHOTOMOUS RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN MAN AND NATURE."

**Panel 5:**
"THAT IS THE STUPIDEST THING I'VE EVER HEARD."

**Panel 6:**
"IT'S LIKE HE SIMPLY BLURTED OUT THE FIRST SEMI-SEMANTICALLY-CORRECT SENTENCE THAT POPPED INTO HIS HEAD, COMPLETELY IGNORING THE FACT THAT IT CONVEYED ABSOLUTELY NOTHING MEANINGFUL."

**Panel 7:**
"HOW MANY COCKTAIL PARTIES HAVE I HAD TO SUFFER THROUGH BY GETTING CAUGHT HAVING TO TALK TO BLOWHARD LIKE HIM?"

**Panel 8:**
"IN FACT, HOW MANY INTERNATIONAL INCIDENTS THROUGHOUT HISTORY HAVE BEEN PRECIPITATED BY CARELESS CHATTERBOXES LIKE HIM?"

**Panel 9:**
"MANY POWERFUL 'INSTITUTIONALIZED ORGANIZATIONS' OFTEN EMPLOY NON-THINKING MOUTHPIECES LIKE HIM TO PROPAGATE THEIR MESSAGES OF HATE AND INTOLERANCE."

**Panel 10:**
"FOR CENTURIES, GOVERNMENTS AND TYRANTS HAVE MANIPULATED USEFUL IDIOTS LIKE HIM TO HELP JUSTIFY TERRORISM, WAR, AND GENOCIDE."

**Panel 11:**
"I MUST PUT A STOP TO THIS NOW BEFORE THIS MONSTER IS RELEASED INTO THE WILD."

**Panel 12:**
"BUT HE'S THE ONLY STUDENT WILLING TO CONTRIBUTE ANYTHING TO THIS DISCUSSION. IF I QUASH HIS ENTHUSIASM NOW, THIS IS GONNA BE ONE HELL OF A BORING CLASS FOR THE REST OF THE SEMESTER."

**Panel 13:**
"HORNS OF A DILEMMA."

**Panel 14:**
"THAT'S A VERY INTERESTING INSIGHT, SEAN. COULD YOU PLEASE ELABORATE?"

**Panel 15:**
(Student looking concerned)

**Panel 16:**
"I AM ONE SMALL COG IN THE VAST MACHINERY OF EVERYTHING WRONG WITH THIS WORLD."

# Visual note

The final panels show the student lying in bed at night in darkness, having spiraled from a simple classroom discussion into existential despair. The contrast between the unspoken internal monologue and the teacher's polite, encouraging responses creates the humor.

# Comic Transcription

## Scene
A minimalist stick-figure comic with nine panels in a 3×3 grid plus one additional panel below. Simple circle faces with minimal details on white backgrounds. One character with a slightly more expressive face argues with three blank-faced listeners, escalating to physical action in the final panel.

## Text

Panel 1:
"I HAVEN'T EATEN RED MEAT IN 1697 DAYS!"

Panel 2:
"I HAVEN'T EATEN POULTRY IN 1588 DAYS AND...
HEY, NOBODY CARES!"

Panel 3:
"WHY DO YOU KEEP REMINDING US EVERYDAY THAT YOU'RE VEGETARIAN?"

Panel 4:
"NOBODY CARES WHAT YOU ATE LAST NIGHT!"

Panel 5:
"NOBODY CARES THAT YOU DON'T EAT STEAK, NOBODY CARES THAT YOU DON'T EAT CHICKEN!"

Panel 6:
"AND NOBODY CARES THAT YOU DON'T EAT FISH OR...
FISH?"

Panel 7:
"I HAVEN'T EATEN FISH IN 1027 DAYS."

Panel 8:
[No text]

Panel 9:
"STUDY: On average, meat-eaters live five years longer than vegetarians."

Final Panel:
[Multiple figures attacking the speaker]
"Scientists baffled."

## Visual note
The humor relies on the contrast between the vegetarian's increasingly ignored boasting and the revelation of a study suggesting meat-eaters live longer, which immediately prompts the other characters to physically attack him.

# Webcomic Transcription

**Scene:** A four-panel comic in black and white line art showing two parallel scenarios. Each features a speaker at a podium addressing a crowd of identical round-headed figures. The art style uses simple, minimalist stick figures. The final panels show newspaper headlines.

**Text:**

Top left panel: "... SO, AS WE DEAL WITH THESE LONG-TERM OPPORTUNITIES TO PROVIDE A MORE ENGAGING GAMING EXPERIENCE, WE ARE ACUTELY AWARE OF THE FRUSTRATION EXPERIENCED BY OUR CUSTOMERS AND ..."

Crowd member: "HEY! GABEN JUST TOUCHED HIS BEARD 3 TIMES!"

Top right panel: "... SO, AS WE ASSESS THE BENEFITS AND COSTS OF ALTERNATIVE POLICY APPROACHES, WE MUST NOT LOSE SIGHT OF THE DAUNTING ECONOMIC CHALLENGES THAT CONFRONT OUR NATION AND ..."

Crowd member: "HEY! BERNANKE JUST TOUCHED HIS BEARD 3 TIMES!"

Middle left panel:
"IT'S A SIGN!"
"YEAH"
"THREE! THREE! THREE!"

Middle right panel:
"IT'S A SIGN!"
"YEAH"
"THREE! THREE! THREE!"

Bottom left panel: "NEWS - HL3 CONFIRMED"

Bottom right panel: "NEWS - QE3 CONFIRMED"

Caption: "The parallels are uncanny."

**Visual note:** Both crowds interpret the same accidental gesture (three beard touches) as confirmation of something significant to their respective domains—gaming and economic policy.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene
A minimalist stick-figure comic showing a character's progression from repeatedly attempting to climb a rope with a hammer, to standing with another character, to standing alone at a cliff edge with hearts floating above, to finally falling and landing at the bottom of a cliff surrounded by friends holding a sign.

## Text
"...but it can be done."

"FRIEND ZONE"

## Visual note
The "friend zone" is depicted as an actual geographic location at the bottom of a cliff, creating a visual pun on the romantic term. The character successfully completes the difficult climb to reach their love interest, only to end up at the bottom of the cliff in the friend zone, suggesting the physical accomplishment did not achieve the romantic goal.

# Webcomic Transcription: "Lucky > Good"

Scene: A minimalist stick-figure comic depicting a race between a person and a rabbit, shown across multiple panels with simple black ink line drawings. The narrative progresses through various terrains and obstacles, culminating in three alternate endings determined by different chance events.

Text: "BANG" / "START" / "ZAP" / "FINISH" / "lucky > good"

Visual note: The rabbit encounters increasingly surreal obstacles (being struck by lightning, caught in rain) while progressing forward, while a slower competitor steadily advances unimpeded. The three final panels show different outcomes based on random events rather than the competitors' abilities, with the faster rabbit suffering misfortune. The comic inverts the traditional race narrative to suggest luck matters more than inherent ability.

# Webcomic Transcription

**Scene:**
A minimalist stick-figure comic with two human characters and a boxy robot with an antenna. The setting is abstract across 12 panels arranged in a 2x6 grid.

**Text:**

Panel 1: "Hey, is that a robot?"

Panel 2: "Good boy." / "CAT"

Panel 3: "Not just any robot. THIS is the current state-of-the-art in artificial intelligence."

Panel 4: "We trained a 9-layered locally connected sparse autoencoder with pooling and local contrast normalization on a dataset of 10 million images. It was trained for 3 days on a cluster of 1000 machines comprising 16,000 cores."

Panel 5: "It can now recognize 22,000 different object categories with 15.8% accuracy."

Panel 6: "15.8%?" / "Impressive, nuh?" / "CAT"

Panel 7: "Impressive? Didn't you AI guys program a machine that defeated a world chess champion way back in 1997?"

Panel 8: "And didn't you AI guys build a computer system that kicked the respective asses of the two best Jeopardy! champions of all time?"

Panel 9: "What happened to 'HAL 9000 by 2001'? Where are our Series 800 Terminator robots?"

Panel 10: "You call this progress?!!"

Panel 11: (Silent panel)

Panel 12: "Sorry we annoyed you with our awesomeness." / "CAT"

**Visual note:**
The robot correctly identifies a cat as "CAT" despite achieving only 15.8% accuracy across 22,000 object categories, contrasting the system's narrow capability with the human's expectations based on AI's previous specialized successes.

comics.title: RSA-2048
comics.comment: If it wasn't for the universe and the laws of physics and stuff, I'd be unstoppable.
comics.commentHtml: If it wasn't for the universe and the laws of physics and stuff, I'd be unstoppable.
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A nine-panel webcomic in black and white stick-figure style showing two characters: one with dark hair at a laptop and one with glasses. The humor centers on the disconnect between theoretical cryptographic success and practical computational reality.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"Hey, check it out. I think I figured out how to crack 2048-bit RSA encryption."

**Panel 2:**
"I can do it using the general number field sieve."

**Panel 3:**
"On that crappy laptop? It would literally take you quadrillions of years!"

**Panel 4:**
"Ahh, but y'see, I'm pretty sure that I'll perfect my time machine within 20 years."

**Panel 5:**
"Then I can just add a function that sends the result back in time after the calculations finish."

**Panel 6:**
"From our point of view, it'll be like we receive the answer as soon as the calculations start. Observe."
START

**Panel 7:**
[Dark-haired character waits at laptop]

**Panel 8:**
"Hmmm. Nothing."

**Panel 9:**
[Empty panel with waiting character]

**Panel 10:**
"I was sure that that was going to work."

**Panel 11:**
"I'll bet the universe ended before the calculations finished."

**Panel 12:**
"Y'know, if it wasn't for real life, you'd be like a genius or something."
"Damn universe!"

# Visual Note

The humor relies on the visual passage of time through repetitive panels showing no results, emphasizing the absurdity of the theoretical solution and the character's increasingly desperate rationalizations for its failure.

A stick-figure comic showing two characters in a room with a table. The art style is deliberately crude with minimal linework.

Panel 2: "They're not gonna miss a few stolen gold coins. Stupid farmers. haha"

Panel 4: "What, why are you looking at me like that?"

Panel 5: "Don't judge me."

Panel 7: "Okay, I'll put it back."

Panel 9: "You're gonna leave me, aren't you?"

Panel 10: "I AM (socially awkward) DRAGONBORN"

# Visual note

The humor depends on the character deflecting from a moral argument about stealing by suddenly making an absurd fantasy reference, revealing their inability to handle social confrontation.

comics.transcript:
# Scene
Three panels show a stick figure sitting at a desk, increasingly frustrated as equations cover the wall behind them. The minimalist line-drawn style emphasizes the character's growing overwhelm across the sequence.

# Text
**Equations on wall (across all three panels):**
E = mc²
E = m²c =
E = m·c
E = m·lose = Emkd
E = c/m = Emcml
E = √mc = E = mc²
E = m%c

**Caption:**
"The best way to have a good idea is to have a lot of ideas.
--- Linus Pauling"

# Visual Note
The character generates increasingly absurd variations of E=mc², degrading into nonsensical formulas like "E = m·lose." The visual progression illustrates Pauling's quote literally—most of the character's generated ideas are completely wrong, demonstrating the cost of quantity in the pursuit of quality.

comics.title: Zarathustra
comics.comment: And Zarathustra spake thus: MY GOD, WE'RE LIVING IN THE FUTURE!
comics.commentHtml: And Zarathustra spake thus: MY GOD, WE'RE LIVING IN THE FUTURE!
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A minimalist stick-figure comic depicting a bearded philosopher descending a mountain to teach a young woman about his philosophy, only to be repeatedly interrupted and distracted by her use of modern technology and social media.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"When Zarathustra was thirty years old, he left his home and the lake of his home, and went into the mountains."

**Panel 2:**
"There he enjoyed his spirit and solitude, and for ten years did not weary of it."

**Panel 3:**
"But at last his heart changed, and rising one morning with the rosy dawn, he went before the sun, and spake thus unto it:"

**Panel 4:**
"Lol! I am weary of my wisdom, like the bee that hath gathered too much honey; I need hands outstretched to take it."

**Panel 5:**
"Zarathustra went down the mountain alone and there suddenly stood before him a young woman."

**Panel 6:**
"And to the young woman Zarathustra spake thus: I TEACH YOU THE SUPERMAN."
Woman: "What?"

**Panel 7:**
Man: "Man is something that is to be surpassed. What have ye done to surpass man?"
Woman: "ok, glass, record a video of this crazy man."

**Panel 8:**
Man: "The Superman is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Superman SHALL BE the meaning of the earth!"
Woman: "ok, glass, upload video to YouTube."

**Panel 9:**
Man: "Are you talking to your glasses?"
Woman: "Yeah. It just took a video of you and uploaded it to the internet. See?"
Man: "Wh-what kind of phone is that?"

**Panel 10:**
Man: "What is all this crazy technology? How did you..."
Woman: "shhh, I'm video chatting with my friends."

**Panel 11:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 12:**
Man: "How long was I on that freakin' mountain?"

**Caption:**
"Thus spake Zarathustra."

# Visual Note
Zarathustra's profound philosophical teachings about the Superman are immediately dismissed and recontextualized as social media content by the woman wearing smart glasses.

comics.title: Einstein
comics.comment: But don't worry. There are thousands of other Einsteins alive on Earth right now about whom you'll also know nothing.
comics.commentHtml: But don't worry.  There are thousands of other Einsteins alive on Earth right now about whom you'll also know nothing.
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A six-panel minimalist webcomic in black and white with two blue panels, depicting a silhouetted figure in various situations. The comic contrasts optimistic opening statements with the harsh realities of poverty and lack of opportunity.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"GOOD NEWS!"
"The next 'Einstein' is alive and living on planet Earth right now."

**Panel 2:**
"She lives in a country nobody ever talks about."
"...in a village nobody cares about."

**Panel 3:**
"She has never read a textbook."
"...and has never seen the inside of a school."

**Panel 4:**
"Every morning she makes the long trek to the market to sell firewood."

**Panel 5:**
"...and every night she makes the long trek back to feed her children."

**Panel 6:**
"You've never heard of her."
"...and you never will."

comics.transcript:
# Scene
A minimalist stick figure comic showing one character repeatedly learning obsolete programming languages while another character offers trendy recommendations. Each language introduction includes a visual hourglass representing its expiration date.

# Text

Panel 1:
Book cover: "THE BLOOP PROGRAMMING LANGUAGE FRAMEWORK"

Panel 2:
Character 1: "Dude, why are you learning BLooP? That's so 2012."
Character 2: "What?"

Panel 3:
Character 1: "Never forget to check the hourglass. See? BLooP has expired."

Panel 4:
Character 1: "Here. Start learning FlooP. All of the hottest startups use FlooP."
Character 2: "oh, okay."

Panel 5:
Character 1: "Good luck."
Character 2: "Thanks."

Panel 6-7:
(Character studying)

Panel 8:
Character 1: "FlooP is so 5 minutes ago."
Character 2: "Yes. LITERALLY 5 minutes ago."

Panel 9:
Character 1: "Here. You want to learn GlooP now."

Panel 10:
Character 1: "And now you have a fresh hourglass again."

Panel 11:
(Character studying)

Panel 12:
Character 2: "Why don't you just tell me what's going to be hot in 6 months?"

Panel 13:
Character 1: "hahah. You're funny."
Character 2: "Dude, why are you learning GlooP?"

# Visual note
The hourglass appears with each programming language, visually representing its rapid expiration and obsolescence as a new framework is immediately recommended as its replacement.

comics.title: Impure Mathematics
comics.comment: There is no branch of mathematics, however abstract, which may not someday be applied to the phenomena of the real world. ---Nicolai Lobachevsky
comics.commentHtml: There is no branch of mathematics, however abstract, which may not someday be applied to the phenomena of the real world. ---Nicolai Lobachevsky
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A black and white stick figure comic in a 3x3 grid. A stick figure with black hair discusses mathematics with a round-faced companion across various scenes, culminating in the character riding a rainbow-colored unicorn. The art style uses minimalist line drawings with occasional text emphasis and visual effects.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"So I arbitrarily defined an arbitrary mathematical structure and arbitrarily gave it some arbitrary features."

**Panel 2:**
"Then I arbitrarily formulated some arbitrary theorems about it."

**Panel 3:**
"What practical applications could this possibly have?"

**Panel 4:**
"There are no practical applications. That's the beauty of pure mathematics."

**Panel 5:**
"YOU !!!" [radiating emphasis lines]
K₍₋ε₎ω05₍₎

**Panel 6:**
"I'm from the future. Come with me if you want to live!"
"What?"

**Panel 7:**
"In the future, your mathematical theories make possible an inter-dimensional rift in the space-time-consciousness continuum which allows Xur and the Ko-Dan armada to conquer the galaxy."
"I don't understand"

**Panel 8:**
"No time to explain. GET ON THE DAMN UNICORN!"

**Panel 9:**
[Character riding rainbow-colored unicorn]

**Bottom caption:**
"All math is applied math... eventually."

# Visual Note

The progression escalates absurdly from an abstract mathematics discussion to a time-traveling warning about intergalactic conquest, culminating in the character riding an unexplained rainbow unicorn. The garbled mathematical notation in Panel 5 emphasizes the chaos of the interruption.

comics.transcript:
# "The Parable of the Computers"

**Scene:** A stick-figure comic presented as a biblical parable. Simple line drawings depict a master character distinguished by a blue cap distributing computers to three servants, then later accounting for what they've done with them.

**Text:**

"For the kingdom of heaven is as a man travelling into a far country, who called his three servants, and delivered unto them his goods."

"And unto his three servants he gave one computer each."
"And straightaway took his journey."

"After a long time the lord of those servants cometh, and reckoneth with them."

"And the lord of the servants said unto the first servant:"
"What have you done with the computer in my absence?"

"Lord, thou deliveredst unto me a computer: behold, I have created a simple and easy-to-use graphical operating system that now runs 90% of the world's personal computers."

"And his lord said unto him: Well done, thou good and faithful servant: thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things."

"Enter thou into the joy of thy lord."
"WOO-HOO"

"And the lord of the servants said unto the second servant:"
"What have you done with the computer in my absence?"

"Lord, thou deliveredst unto me a computer: behold, I have created a social networking site that now has over a billion users worldwide."

"And his lord said unto him: Well done, thou good and faithful servant: thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things."

"Enter thou into the joy of thy lord."
"yee-ha"

"And the lord of the servants said unto the third servant:"
"And what have you done with the computer in my absence?"

[The third servant sits at a desk with a laptop, unresponsive.]

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A minimalist stick-figure webcomic depicting a narrator's experience as support staff in academic research. The comic uses medieval knight metaphors contrasted with laboratory settings to illustrate the invisible labor behind scientific discovery.

# Text

"You may not always see us but we are always there."

"We are the beleaguered squires toiling in the backdrop while the glorious knights in burnished armor take to the calamitous battlefield and pierce the dark forces of ignorance with bitter spears of reason."

"Brandishing elegant tools of empirical illumination, they drive back the wavering darkness of superstition."

"...bringing to bear the full might of logic and deductive reasoning... And we are there."

"Many battles have been fought and many battles have been won as they engird their foes in an 'ever-receding pocket of scientific ignorance'* - and we are there."

"*NJT"

"And when tales of their victories are put to paper and added to the esteemed archives of academia, our names you will see amongst those of the scholarly knights of lore."

"Some Great Scientific Discovery"

Authors: "Polly Postdoc, Gary Graduatedstud, Obligatory Asian Dude, Larry Labtech, [ME], Pricilla Principlesinvestigator"

Abstract: "We made some great scientific discovery."

Introduction: "Blah blah blah [repeated placeholder text]"

"You may not always see us but we are always there. We are indispensable journeymen in the grand adventure of scientific discovery. We are the squires of the academic knights."

"We are the test tube washers"

# Visual note

The narrator's name in the author list requires an arrow to locate, visually reinforcing the theme of being present yet overlooked.

comics.title: The Story of the Cheese Maze
comics.comment: And the rats loved those books even more.
comics.commentHtml: And the rats loved those books even more.
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A simple black-and-white comic strip featuring four characters in a maze: two rats named Avelli and Machi, and two small humanoid creatures named Hill and Napoleon. The art style uses minimal line drawings and simple rectangular panels with occasional larger illustrated scenes.

# Text

"Once, long ago in a land far away, there lived four little characters who ran through a maze looking for cheese to make them happy."

"Two were rats named 'Avelli' and 'Machi' and two were littlepeople - beings who were as small as rats but who looked and acted a lot like people today. Their names were 'Hill' and 'Napoleon'."

"Every day the four of them spent like little mice searching for their own special cheese."

"Then one day, they each found their own kind of cheese at the end of one of the corridors."

"And every morning after that, the rats and the littlepeople headed directly to the same corridor to find the good good cheese."

"Until one day, the rats approached the littlepeople, pounded their tiny little bitch asses into the ground, and stole their cheese."

"The rats used their newfound cheese wealth to build a machine that could create cheese out of thin air."

"The machine churned out so much good good cheese, so much cheese: glorious mountains of Swiss cheese, soaring towers of Cheddar cheese, gushing rivers of delicious cheese Whiz."

"And the rats built a huge wall around their cheese to keep out the littlepeople."

"Sometimes the rats threw pieces of cheese over the wall. And they laughed and laughed while voraciously sucking their cheese Callahes as the littlepeople fought and clawed for every tiny morsel."

"So the littlepeople learned to read - books that explained how anyone can get all the cheese they wanted if they worked hard and had a positive attitude."

"The littlepeople loved those books."

"THE END"

# Visual note

The contrast between the simple, cute character designs and the violent description creates dark comedy through juxtaposition. The illustrated cheese mountains and towers emphasize grotesque excess. The wall separating the rats from the littlepeople visually conveys the hopelessness of the situation, making the conclusion about positive attitudes absurdly inadequate.

comics.title: alone in my room
comics.comment: And attack ships on fire off the shoulder of Orion.
comics.commentHtml: And attack ships on fire off the shoulder of Orion.
comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription

**Scene:**
A minimalist black and white comic featuring a young person with short dark hair alone in their room, surrounded by mathematical equations and abstract imagery. The panels alternate between simple line drawings and detailed sketches, depicting both mundane indoor settings and surreal cosmic landscapes.

**Text:**

"Alone in my room. scribbling equations."

"I've... seen things. ...things you wouldn't believe."

"...terrible, beautiful things quivering on the horizon of my mind's eye."

"I have seen the cold and austere universe of randomness dance in rhythm with the music of the primes at the edge of infinity."

"I have run barefoot along the sinewy embankments of countless Calabi-Tau manifolds."

"I have soared naked through the magnificent solitude of infinite dimensional Hilbert space and through exotic realms that have no business existing in the mundane abstractions of mortal men."

"With surgical precision, I have weft, stitched, and knotted together the obscene contortions that would make angels weep."

"I have collapsed stars into monstrous vortices from which nothing can escape and I have blithely skated along its pristine event horizon."

"I have witnessed the birth and death of this and many other universes many times. So it goes."

"Alone in my room."

"With nothing but the formidable lens of mathematics and a fecundity of imagination...."

"I've seen some shit."

**Visual note:**
The comic contrasts elaborate descriptions of cosmic experiences with the revelation that all these phenomena exist only through mathematics and imagination in a solitary room, culminating in crude understatement.

comics.title: pseudonymous man of mystery
comics.comment: I'm pretty sure that the real Satoshi Nakamoto would be a supreme disappointment compared to the image I've built up in my mind.
comics.commentHtml: I'm pretty sure that the real Satoshi Nakamoto would be a supreme disappointment compared to the image I've built up in my mind.
comics.transcript:
# Scene

A stick-figure webcomic on white background depicting a first-person character in various indoor scenes, concluding with a nighttime cityscape. The narrative uses simple black line drawings and an irreverent tone.

# Text

"Yes, it's true that I once forged a sword of awesomeness out of chunks of meteorites."

"And yes, I once killed a ferocious bear with my bare hands to save a basket of kittens."

"And every year on April 20, I make sweet, sweet love to a beautiful mermaid on a beach while wearing a dead bear's skin."

"I didn't have to use my bare hands to kill that bear because I, of course, shoot deadly lasers from my ass if I want to."

"But I'm a private person. I live alone in my lair of solitude atop my own personal Mount Olympus."

"And in my lair, I bide my time by wisely monitoring the progress of humanity."

"On occasion, when I have found that humanity was stuck in a stalemate or suffering from a stale status quo bias, I have descended from my hiding place."

"...and imparted my Promethean gift of disruptive innovation to the masses."

"And then, as quickly as I came, I would disappear into the dark of night like a ghost in an Audi 5000."

"Out of sight from the attention of civilisation once more, I am again free to monitor the progress of humanity."

"And if the people of earth should ever require my services again..."

"I, Satoshi Nakamoto, shall return."

# Visual Note

The final panel reveals a Bitcoin symbol in a spotlight beam above a nighttime cityscape.

# Scene
A stick-figure comic in black and white showing a character with wild, spiky hair standing before three seated audience members. The character's emotional state becomes increasingly frantic across the panels.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
F = G(m₁m₂)/r²

**Panel 2:**
I CALL IT THE LAW OF UNIVERSAL GRAVITATION.

**Panel 3:**
Y'KNOW, IT WOULD BE REALLY COOL IF, LIKE, SOMEONE COULD FIND A PRECISE VALUE FOR...

**Panel 5:**
DID YOU HEAR WHAT I SAID?

**Panel 6:**
YES, M'LORD.

**Panel 7:**
THEN I MEAN NOW! MOVE!

**Panel 9:**
EXPERIMENTALISTS.

# Visual Note
The humor derives from the contrast between the speaker's academic excitement about physics and their immediate, imperious demand for action, treating the audience members like servants rather than fellow scientists. The final dismissal of experimentalists suggests frustration with those who must perform the tedious practical work to validate theoretical predictions.

comics.transcript:
# Scene

A multi-panel webcomic in simple black and white stick figure style. A professor teaches quantum mechanics to a small class, but is derailed by one student's increasingly elaborate philosophical questions. The final panel shows the professor reassigned to teach Philosophy 101 to a much larger lecture hall.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"So these are the basic postulates of quantum mechanics that we'll be working with this semester."

[Board: "ℏ∂ψ/∂t=-ℏ²/2m∇²ψ + V(x)ψ"]

**Panel 2:**
"Are there any questions so far?"

**Panel 3:**
"Yes, Sean?"

**Panel 4:**
"So are you saying that quantum phenomena don't exist without the presence of a conscious observer?"

**Panel 5:**
"Uhh... no, that's not exactly what..."

"And you seem to be implying that the mathematical abstractions used to describe the processes are more real than the objects themselves."

**Panel 6:**
"I think you misunderstood what I..."

"And isn't mathematics just a man-made construct constrained by the physiological limitations of human cognitive abilities?"

**Panel 7:**
"Well, mathematics is just a tool that..."

"What if an alien from the Andromeda galaxy with a vastly different brain structure, and hence system of mathematics, were to develop a quantum theory based on their own mathematics? Couldn't their conception of reality possibly be completely different from our own? Who's to say that their version of reality isn't more real than ours? In fact, what is real? Maybe we should come up with a new definition of reality."

**Panel 8:**
[Silent panel]

**Panel 9:**
"I can see that there's nothing more that I can teach you."

**Panel 10:**
"I believe that you're ready for our advanced class."

**Panel 11:**
[Transition]

**Panel 12:**
[Classroom sign: "PHILOSOPHY 101"]

[Professor stands at lectern facing a large lecture hall of students]

# Visual note

Sean's philosophical questions grow progressively longer and more absurd across three consecutive panels. The professor's "reward" for finding an advanced student is reassignment from a small quantum mechanics class to a crowded Philosophy 101 lecture hall, suggesting philosophical inquiry attracts far larger and more chaotic audiences than rigorous physics.

# Scene

A nine-panel stick-figure comic showing a character at a desk experiencing escalating financial delusions and power fantasies, which are abruptly deflated by a humbling reality check. The art style is minimalist black-and-white line drawings with simple circular faces.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"BUY 5000 MARCH 50 CALLS, START BUYING 10,000 SHARE BLOCKS AND TAKE IT UP TO 50 DOLLARS."

**Panel 2:**
"ANOTHER PHONE CALL. ANOTHER MILLION DOLLARS."

**Panel 3:**
"WITH A SINGLE PHONE CALL, I CAN MOVE WORLD MARKETS. I CAN MAKE OR BREAK ENTIRE INDUSTRIES OR CORPORATIONS."

**Panel 4:**
"WITH A CLICK OF A MOUSE, I CAN INSTALL WORLD LEADERS OR COLLAPSE SMALL GOVERNMENTS."

**Panel 5:**
"I HOLD THE FATE OF MILLIONS OF PEASANTS IN MY HAND."

**Panel 6:**
"I AM A LORD OF FINANCE. I AM THE WIZARD OF WALL STREET."

**Panel 7:**
"I AM MASTER OF THE UNIVERSE!"

**Panel 8:**
"SIR, DON'T FORGET YOUR DOCTOR'S APPOINTMENT AT 3 O'CLOCK."

**Panel 9:**
"I WILL NOW UNCEREMONIOUSLY STICK MY FREAKISHLY LARGE FINGER UP YOUR BUTT."

**Caption:**
"THE PROSTATE EXAM — the great equalizer of all men"

# Visual Note

The character's grandiose monologue about financial and political power is undercut by an administrative reminder, leading to a medical procedure that strips away all pretense of control or dignity.

# The Global Brain

**Scene:**
A multi-panel webcomic in simple line-drawing style showing two stick figures interacting with an elaborate neural network visualization that expands into a massive brain structure. The imagery progresses from a mundane transaction to an absurdist, cosmic bureaucratic scenario.

**Text:**
Panel 1: "swipe card"

Panel 3: "...processing..."

"SIR, CITIZEN #168-37118-3141 IS ATTEMPTING TO MAKE A PURCHASE OF $37.98."

"I WILL ALLOW IT."

"approved"

"SNOOPY DANCE"

"The Global Brain has approved your purchase"

**Visual note:**
A routine $37.98 purchase requires approval from an omniscient cosmic entity visualized as an elaborate neural network and massive brain, juxtaposing mundane bureaucracy with surreal, god-like intelligence. The transaction's approval is celebrated with a lighthearted "SNOOPY DANCE," providing absurdist humor through the contrast between the grandiose approval process and the trivial purchase amount.

# Scene

A nine-panel stick figure webcomic in black and white showing two characters in a bedroom. One character has long dark hair; the other is round-headed and wearing blue. The conversation escalates from a discussion about spicing up their intimate life into increasingly absurd and painful suggestions, ending with a letter to a magazine.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
"Hey, what's with all this equipment in the bedroom?"

**Panel 2:**
"I thought I'd spice up our sex life."

**Panel 3:**
"Okaaay... but why do you have a paintball gun? It's filled with Sriracha sauce."

**Panel 4:**
"I thought it would be nice to shoot the sauce up your nose right before you climax."
"WHAT?"

**Panel 5:**
"Why? Why would you do that?! Why? Why?"
"To enhance the pleasure."

**Panel 6:**
"Whose pleasure?"

**Panel 7:**
"And then I'm gonna snap this mousetrap on your balls"
"Are you crazy?"

**Panel 8:**
"But first I'm gonna twist your penis like a pretzel around this hot curling iron."

**Panel 9:**
"C'mere, you. Time to be pleasured."
"Dear, God! Make it all stop."

**Letter:**
"Dear Cosmo,
Yeah, if you could stop giving sex tips to our girlfriends, that'd be great.

Sincerely,
MEN"

# Visual Note

The humor contrasts the graphic and painful nature of the proposed acts with the simplistic stick figure art style, and concludes by attributing these suggestions to Cosmo magazine's sex advice column.

# Scene

A minimalist black and white comic depicting a monkey and a blue sphere character at a zoo. The eight panels are arranged in a 4x2 grid, progressing through an increasingly heated conversation.

# Text

**Panel 1:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 2:**
Monkey: "Hey, buddy, why don't you take a picture? It'll last longer."
Blue sphere: "What?"

**Panel 3:**
Blue sphere: "You think you're pretty hot stuff, don't you? How noble in reason, how infinite in faculty!"
Blue sphere: "?"

**Panel 4:**
Monkey: "Don't you look down on me, you *Homo sapiens* sumbabitch. Genetically, we're practically identical."

**Panel 5:**
Monkey: "What if certain random genetic mutations and millions of years of cumulative natural selection turned out just a little bit differently?"

**Panel 6:**
Monkey: "It could have been you with the measly 400 cc cranial capacity sitting here in this cramped prison masturbating in public and throwing poo. But nooooooooo..."

**Panel 7:**
Monkey: "Look at you, with your 1400 cc brain living in your big, fancy house in the suburbs listening to Mozart and reading Shakespeare getting all fat off of the fruits of your magnificent technological innovations."

**Panel 8:**
Monkey: "You think I'm an evolutionary failure, don't you?"
Blue sphere: "DON'T YOU?!!"
Blue sphere: "I just wanted to see some monkeys."

**Caption:**
"zoos make me sad"

# Visual note

The monkey's elaborate philosophical tirade about evolutionary contingency and human superiority is triggered by the blue sphere simply taking photographs, making the intensity of the response absurdly disproportionate to the innocent action.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene
A six-panel stick figure comic depicting a conversation between two people sitting at a dining table. The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white background.

## Text

**Panel 1:**
Woman: "I don't think I'm getting enough mathematical stimulation from his relationship."
Man: "What are you saying?"

**Panel 2:**
Woman: "I'm having an affair with your brother Johann."
Man: "What?! But I'm Johann."

**Panel 3:**
Woman: "You are? Oh, then I guess I meant to say Jacob. When did you start sleeping with Jacob?!!"

**Panel 4:**
Man: "Right after I broke it off with Daniel."
Woman: "You slept with my brother Daniel too!!"

**Panel 5:**
Woman: "Brother? No, I slept with your *uncle Daniel*."
Man: "Wait... What?"

**Panel 6:**
Woman: "Sorry. I keep getting all you Bernoullis mixed up."
Man: (silent, frustrated reaction)

## Visual Note
The punchline reveals that all the men involved are members of the Bernoulli family, a Swiss mathematical dynasty, which connects to the opening reference to "mathematical stimulation."
